**Milwaukee**[[a]](#cite_note-11) is the [most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wisconsin "List of cities in Wisconsin") in the U.S. state of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). It is located on the western shore of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") at the confluence of the [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"), [Menomonee](/wiki/Menomonee_River "Menomonee River"), and [Kinnickinnic](/wiki/Kinnickinnic_River_(Milwaukee_River) "Kinnickinnic River (Milwaukee River)") Rivers. Milwaukee is the [31st-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") in the United States and the fifth-most populous city in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), with a population of 577,222 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"). The [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area") has over 1.57 million residents and ranks as the [40th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the nation. It is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Milwaukee County, Wisconsin").[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Present-day Milwaukee was inhabited by various [Algonquian peoples](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples"). Following the [1833 Treaty of Chicago](/wiki/1833_Treaty_of_Chicago "1833 Treaty of Chicago"), European settlers established the city as a hub for trade and industry by capitalizing on its location as a [port](/wiki/Port "Port"). Milwaukee was incorporated in 1846. The city attracted immigrants from Germany and elsewhere in Europe, and Milwaukee remains a [center of German American culture](/wiki/Germans_in_Milwaukee "Germans in Milwaukee"). In the late-19th and 20th centuries, Milwaukee became known as a [national leader in beer production](/wiki/Beer_in_Milwaukee "Beer in Milwaukee") led by the [Miller](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company"), [Pabst](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company"), and [Schlitz](/wiki/Joseph_Schlitz_Brewing_Company "Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company") breweries. The city also became known for [its strong labor movement](/wiki/Sewer_socialism "Sewer socialism").

Milwaukee is an ethnically and [culturally diverse](/wiki/Culturally_diverse "Culturally diverse") city,[[12]](#cite_note-13) though it continues to be one of the most racially segregated cities in the U.S. as a result of early-20th century [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining").[[13]](#cite_note-Leah_Foltman_&_Malia_Jones-14) Its [regional GDP](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") was over $130 billion in 2023.[[14]](#cite_note-15) The city is home to [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies [Northwestern Mutual](/wiki/Northwestern_Mutual "Northwestern Mutual"), [Fiserv](/wiki/Fiserv "Fiserv"), [ManpowerGroup](/wiki/ManpowerGroup "ManpowerGroup"), [Rockwell Automation](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation "Rockwell Automation"), and [WEC Energy Group](/wiki/WEC_Energy_Group "WEC Energy Group").[[15]](#cite_note-fortune-16) Its cultural institutions include the [Harley-Davidson Museum](/wiki/Harley-Davidson_Museum "Harley-Davidson Museum"), [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum"), [Milwaukee Public Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Museum "Milwaukee Public Museum"), and [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest"), one of the world's largest music festivals. It is home to several institutions of higher education, including [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University"), [Milwaukee School of Engineering](/wiki/Milwaukee_School_of_Engineering "Milwaukee School of Engineering"), and the [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee"). The city's major [professional sports teams](/wiki/Sports_in_Milwaukee "Sports in Milwaukee") are the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") ([MLB](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball")) and [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") ([NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association")).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Milwaukee](/wiki/History_of_Milwaukee "History of Milwaukee")

### Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Name")]

The etymological origin of the name *Milwaukee* is disputed.[[16]](#cite_note-MilMagMilwaukeeMean-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18) Wisconsin academic Virgil J. Vogel has said, "the name [...] Milwaukee is not difficult to explain, yet there are a number of conflicting claims made concerning it."[[18]](#cite_note-Vogel134-19)

One theory says it comes from the [Anishinaabemowin/Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language") word *mino-akking*, meaning "good land",[[16]](#cite_note-MilMagMilwaukeeMean-17)[[19]](#cite_note-WUWM_origin-20) or words in closely related languages that mean the same.[[20]](#cite_note-21) These included Menominee and Potawatomi.[[18]](#cite_note-Vogel134-19) This theory was popularized by a line by [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") in the 1992 comedy film *[Wayne's World](/wiki/Wayne%27s_World_(film) "Wayne's World (film)")*.[[16]](#cite_note-MilMagMilwaukeeMean-17) Another theory is that it stems from the [Meskwaki](/wiki/Fox_language "Fox language") language, whose term for "gathering place" is *mahn-a-waukee*.[[16]](#cite_note-MilMagMilwaukeeMean-17)[[19]](#cite_note-WUWM_origin-20) The city of Milwaukee itself claims that the name is derived from *mahn-ah-wauk*, a Potawatomi word for "council grounds".[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The name of the future city was spelled in many ways prior to 1844.[[22]](#cite_note-Legler-23) People living west of the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") preferred the modern-day spelling, while those east of the river often called it *Milwaukie*.[[16]](#cite_note-MilMagMilwaukeeMean-17) Other spellings included *Melleokii* (1679), *Millioki* (1679), *Meleki* (1684), *Milwarik* (1699), *Milwacky* (1761), *Milwakie* (1779), *Millewackie* (1817), *Milwahkie* (1820), and *Milwalky* (1821). The *[Milwaukee Sentinel](/wiki/Milwaukee_Sentinel "Milwaukee Sentinel")* used *Milwaukie* in its headline until it switched to *Milwaukee* on November 30, 1844.[[22]](#cite_note-Legler-23) The city of [Milwaukie, Oregon](/wiki/Milwaukie,_Oregon "Milwaukie, Oregon"), retains the alternate spelling.

### Indigenous cultures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Indigenous cultures")]

Indigenous cultures lived along the waterways for thousands of years. The first recorded inhabitants of the Milwaukee area were various [Native American](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") tribes: the [Menominee](/wiki/Menominee "Menominee"), [Meskwaki](/wiki/Meskwaki "Meskwaki"), [Mascouten](/wiki/Mascouten "Mascouten"), [Sauk](/wiki/Sauk_people "Sauk people"), [Potawatomi](/wiki/Potawatomi "Potawatomi"), and [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe "Ojibwe") (all Algic/Algonquian peoples), and the [Ho-Chunk](/wiki/Ho-Chunk "Ho-Chunk") (Winnebago, a Siouan people). Many of these people had lived around [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin")[[23]](#cite_note-24) before migrating to the Milwaukee area at about the time of European contact.

In the second half of the 18th century, the Native Americans living near Milwaukee played a role in all the major European wars on the American continent. During the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"), a group of "Ojibwas and Pottawattamies from the far [Lake] Michigan" (i.e., the area from Milwaukee to Green Bay) joined the French-Canadian [Daniel Liénard de Beaujeu](/wiki/Daniel_Li%C3%A9nard_de_Beaujeu "Daniel Liénard de Beaujeu") at the [Battle of the Monongahela](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Monongahela "Battle of the Monongahela").[[24]](#cite_note-25) In the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the Native Americans around Milwaukee were some of the few groups to ally with the rebel Continentals.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

After the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), the Native Americans fought the United States in the [Northwest Indian War](/wiki/Northwest_Indian_War "Northwest Indian War") as part of the [Council of Three Fires](/wiki/Council_of_Three_Fires "Council of Three Fires"). During the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), they held a council in Milwaukee in June 1812, which resulted in their decision to attack [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago")[[26]](#cite_note-27) in retaliation against American expansion. This resulted in the [Battle of Fort Dearborn](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Dearborn "Battle of Fort Dearborn") on August 15, 1812, the only known armed conflict in Chicago. This battle convinced the American government to [remove](/wiki/Indian_Removal "Indian Removal") these groups of Native Americans from their indigenous land.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Milwaukee#Dubious "Talk:Milwaukee")*] After being attacked in the [Black Hawk War](/wiki/Black_Hawk_War "Black Hawk War") in 1832, the Native Americans in Milwaukee signed the [1833 Treaty of Chicago](/wiki/1833_Treaty_of_Chicago "1833 Treaty of Chicago") with the United States. In exchange for ceding their lands in the area, they were to receive monetary payments and lands west of the Mississippi in [Indian Territory](/wiki/Indian_Territory "Indian Territory").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

### European settlement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: European settlement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Solomon_Juneau.jpg/250px-Solomon_Juneau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Solomon_Juneau.jpg)

Statue of [Solomon Juneau](/wiki/Solomon_Juneau "Solomon Juneau"), who helped establish the city of Milwaukee

Europeans arrived in the Milwaukee area before the 1833 Treaty of Chicago. French missionaries and traders first passed through the area in the late 17th and 18th centuries. Alexis Laframboise, coming from Michilimackinac (now in Michigan), settled a trading post in 1785 and is considered the first resident of European descent in the Milwaukee region.[[28]](#cite_note-St-Pierre,_T_1895-29)

One story on the origin of Milwaukee's name says,

> [O]ne day during the thirties of the last century [1800s] a newspaper calmly changed the name to Milwaukee, and Milwaukee it has remained until this day.[[29]](#cite_note-WGBruce-30)

The spelling "Milwaukie" lives on in [Milwaukie, Oregon](/wiki/Milwaukie,_Oregon "Milwaukie, Oregon"), named after the Wisconsin city in 1847, before the current spelling was universally accepted.[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Milwaukee has three "[founding fathers](/wiki/Father_of_the_Nation "Father of the Nation")": [Solomon Juneau](/wiki/Solomon_Juneau "Solomon Juneau"), [Byron Kilbourn](/wiki/Byron_Kilbourn "Byron Kilbourn"), and [George H. Walker](/wiki/George_H._Walker "George H. Walker"). Solomon Juneau was the first of the three to come to the area, in 1818. He founded a town called Juneau's Side, or Juneautown, that began attracting more settlers. In competition with Juneau, Byron Kilbourn established Kilbourntown west of the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"). He ensured the roads running toward the river did not join with those on the east side. This accounts for the large number of angled bridges that still exist in Milwaukee today.[[31]](#cite_note-32) Further, Kilbourn distributed maps of the area which only showed Kilbourntown, implying Juneautown did not exist or the river's east side was uninhabited and thus undesirable. The third prominent developer was George H. Walker. He claimed land to the south of the Milwaukee River, along with Juneautown, where he built a log house in 1834. This area grew and became known as Walker's Point.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The first large wave of settlement to the areas that would later become Milwaukee County and the City of Milwaukee began in 1835, following removal of the tribes in the Council of Three Fires. Early that year it became known that Juneau and Kilbourn intended to lay out competing town-sites. By the year's end both had purchased their lands from the government and made their first sales. There were perhaps 100 new settlers in this year, mostly from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and other Eastern states. On September 17, 1835, the first election was held in Milwaukee; the number of votes cast was 39.[[33]](#cite_note-34)

By 1840, the three towns had grown, along with their rivalries. There were intense battles between the towns, mainly Juneautown and Kilbourntown, which culminated with the [Milwaukee Bridge War](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bridge_War "Milwaukee Bridge War") of 1845. Following the Bridge War, on January 31, 1846, the towns were combined to incorporate as the City of Milwaukee, and elected Solomon Juneau as Milwaukee's first mayor.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

### Growth and immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Growth and immigration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Milwaukee_birdseye_map_by_Bailey_%281872%29._loc_call_no_g4124m-pm010450.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_birdseye_map_by_Bailey_%281872%29._loc_call_no_g4124m-pm010450.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_birdseye_map_by_Bailey_(1872)._loc_call_no_g4124m-pm010450.jpg)

Illustrated map of Milwaukee in 1872

Milwaukee began to grow as a city as high numbers of immigrants, mainly [German](/wiki/Germans "Germans"), made their way to Wisconsin during the 1840s and 1850s.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Scholars classify [German immigration to the United States](/wiki/German_immigration_to_the_United_States "German immigration to the United States") in three major waves, and Wisconsin received a significant number of immigrants from all three. The first wave from 1845 to 1855 consisted mainly of people from [Southwestern Germany](/wiki/Southwestern_Germany "Southwestern Germany"), the second wave from 1865 to 1873 concerned primarily [Northwestern Germany](/wiki/Northwestern_Germany "Northwestern Germany"), while the third wave from 1880 to 1893 came from [Northeastern Germany](/wiki/Northeastern_Germany "Northeastern Germany").[[36]](#cite_note-Bungert,_Heike_2006-37) By 1900, 34 percent of Milwaukee's population was of German background.[[36]](#cite_note-Bungert,_Heike_2006-37) The largest number of German immigrants to Milwaukee came from [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"), followed by [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony"), [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), and [Hesse-Darmstadt](/wiki/Hesse-Darmstadt "Hesse-Darmstadt"). Milwaukee gained its reputation as the most German of American cities not just from the large number of German immigrants it received, but also for the sense of community that the immigrants established.[[37]](#cite_note-Conzen,_Kathleen_Neils_1860-38)

Most German immigrants came to Wisconsin in search of inexpensive farmland.[[37]](#cite_note-Conzen,_Kathleen_Neils_1860-38) However, immigration began to change in character and size in the late 1840s and early 1850s, due to the [1848 revolutionary movements in Europe](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848 "Revolutions of 1848").[[38]](#cite_note-39) After 1848, hopes for a united Germany had failed, and revolutionary and radical Germans, known as the "[Forty-Eighters](/wiki/Forty-Eighters "Forty-Eighters")", immigrated to the U.S. to avoid imprisonment and persecution by German authorities.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

One of the most famous "liberal revolutionaries" of 1848 was [Carl Schurz](/wiki/Carl_Schurz "Carl Schurz"). He later explained in 1854 why he came to Milwaukee,

> "It is true, similar things [cultural events and societies] were done in other cities where the Forty-eighters [*[sic](/wiki/Sic "Sic")*] had congregated. But so far as I know, nowhere did their influence so quickly impress itself upon the whole social atmosphere as in 'German Athens of America' as Milwaukee was called at the time."[[40]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Lake_Front_Depot_1898_LOC_ds.00203.jpg/250px-Lake_Front_Depot_1898_LOC_ds.00203.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lake_Front_Depot_1898_LOC_ds.00203.jpg)

Milwaukee's [Lake Front Depot](/wiki/Lake_Front_Depot "Lake Front Depot") in 1898

Schurz was referring to the various clubs and societies Germans developed in Milwaukee. The American [Turners](/wiki/Turners "Turners") established its own [Normal College](/wiki/Normal_College "Normal College") for teachers of physical education and the [German-English Academy](/wiki/University_School_of_Milwaukee "University School of Milwaukee").[[41]](#cite_note-42) Milwaukee's German element is still strongly present; the city celebrates its German culture by annually hosting a German Fest in July[[42]](#cite_note-43) and an [Oktoberfest](/wiki/Oktoberfest "Oktoberfest") in October. Milwaukee boasts a number of German restaurants, as well as a traditional German beer hall. A German language [immersion school](/wiki/Immersion_school "Immersion school") is offered for children in grades [K–5](/wiki/K-5_(education) "K-5 (education)").[[43]](#cite_note-immersionschool-44)

Although the German presence in Milwaukee after the Civil War remained strong and their largest wave of immigrants had yet to land, other groups also made their way to the city. Foremost among these were [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") immigrants. Because Milwaukee offered the Polish immigrants an abundance of low-paying entry-level jobs, it became home to [one of the largest Polish-American communities](/wiki/List_of_the_United_States_cities_with_large_Polish-American_populations "List of the United States cities with large Polish-American populations").[[44]](#cite_note-45) For many residents, [Milwaukee's South Side](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee#South_Side "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee") is synonymous with the Polish community.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Milwaukee County's Polish population of 30,000 in 1890 rose to 100,000 by 1915.[[46]](#cite_note-47) [St. Stanislaus Catholic Church](/wiki/St._Stanislaus_Catholic_Church_(Milwaukee,_Wisconsin) "St. Stanislaus Catholic Church (Milwaukee, Wisconsin)") and the surrounding [neighborhood](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee") was the center of [Polish](/wiki/Polish_people "Polish people") life in Milwaukee. As the Polish community surrounding St. Stanislaus continued to grow, Mitchell Street became known as the "Polish Grand Avenue". As Mitchell Street grew more dense, the Polish population started moving south to the [Lincoln Village neighborhood](/wiki/Lincoln_Village,_City_of_Milwaukee,_Wisconsin "Lincoln Village, City of Milwaukee, Wisconsin"), home to the [Basilica of St. Josaphat](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._Josaphat "Basilica of St. Josaphat") and [Kosciuszko Park](/wiki/Lincoln_Village,_City_of_Milwaukee,_Wisconsin#Kosciuszko_Park "Lincoln Village, City of Milwaukee, Wisconsin"). Other Polish communities started on [the East Side of Milwaukee](/wiki/The_East_Side_(Milwaukee) "The East Side (Milwaukee)"). [Jones Island](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee#Jones_Island "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee") was a major [commercial fishing](/wiki/Commercial_fishing "Commercial fishing") center settled mostly by [Kashubians](/wiki/Kashubians "Kashubians") and other Poles from around the [Baltic Sea](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").[[47]](#cite_note-48) Milwaukee has the fifth-largest Polish population in the U.S. at 45,467, ranking behind [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") (211,203), [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") (165,784), [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles") (60,316) and [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") (52,648).[[48]](#cite_note-factfinder2.census.gov-49) The city holds [Polish Fest](/wiki/Polish_Fest "Polish Fest"), an annual celebration of [Polish culture](/wiki/Polish_culture "Polish culture") and [cuisine](/wiki/Polish_food "Polish food").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Pabst_Building_Milwaukee_from_LOC_ID_Service-pnp-det-4a00000-4a08000-4a08000-4a08079v.jpg/250px-Pabst_Building_Milwaukee_from_LOC_ID_Service-pnp-det-4a00000-4a08000-4a08000-4a08079v.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pabst_Building_Milwaukee_from_LOC_ID_Service-pnp-det-4a00000-4a08000-4a08000-4a08079v.jpg)

Wisconsin Street and the [Pabst Building](/wiki/Pabst_Building "Pabst Building") in the early 20th century

In addition to the Germans and Poles, Milwaukee received a large influx of other [European](/wiki/Europe "Europe") immigrants from [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia"), and [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), who included [Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews"), [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutherans "Lutherans"), and [Catholics](/wiki/Catholics "Catholics"). [Italian Americans](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") total 16,992 in the city, but in Milwaukee County, they number at 38,286.[[48]](#cite_note-factfinder2.census.gov-49) The largest Italian-American festival in the area, *Festa Italiana*, is held in the city, while *Irishfest* is the largest Irish-American festival in southeast Wisconsin.[[50]](#cite_note-51) By 1910, Milwaukee shared the distinction with [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") of having the largest percentage of foreign-born residents in the United States.[[51]](#cite_note-52) In 1910, European descendants ("Whites") represented 99.7% of the city's total population of 373,857.[[52]](#cite_note-53) Milwaukee has a strong [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox "Greek Orthodox") Community, many of whom attend the [Annunciation Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Annunciation_Greek_Orthodox_Church "Annunciation Greek Orthodox Church") on Milwaukee's northwest side, designed by Wisconsin-born architect [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright"). Milwaukee has a sizable [Croatian](/wiki/Croats "Croats") population, with Croatian churches and their own historic and successful soccer club [The Croatian Eagles](/wiki/Croatian_Eagles "Croatian Eagles") at Croatian Park in Franklin, Wisconsin.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

Milwaukee also has a large [Serbian](/wiki/Serbs "Serbs") population, who have developed Serbian restaurants, a [Serbian K–8 School](/wiki/St._Sava_Orthodox_School "St. Sava Orthodox School"), and Serbian churches, along with an American Serb Hall. The American Serb Hall in Milwaukee is known for its Friday fish fries and popular events. Many U.S. presidents have visited Milwaukee's Serb Hall in the past.[[54]](#cite_note-55) The Bosnian population is growing in Milwaukee as well due to late-20th-century immigration after the war in [Bosnia-Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia-Herzegovina "Bosnia-Herzegovina").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During this time, a small community of [African Americans](/wiki/African_American "African American") migrated from the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") in the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)"). They settled near each other, forming a community that came to be known as [Bronzeville](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee#Bronzeville "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee"). As industry boomed, more migrants came, and African-American influence grew in Milwaukee.[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In 1892, [Whitefish Bay](/wiki/Whitefish_Bay,_Wisconsin "Whitefish Bay, Wisconsin"), [South Milwaukee](/wiki/South_Milwaukee "South Milwaukee"), and [Wauwatosa](/wiki/Wauwatosa "Wauwatosa") were incorporated. They were followed by [Cudahy](/wiki/Cudahy,_Wisconsin "Cudahy, Wisconsin") (1895), North Milwaukee (1897) and East Milwaukee, later known as [Shorewood](/wiki/Shorewood,_Wisconsin "Shorewood, Wisconsin"), in 1900. In the early 20th century, [West Allis](/wiki/West_Allis "West Allis") (1902), and [West Milwaukee](/wiki/West_Milwaukee "West Milwaukee") (1906) were added, which completed the first generation of "inner-ring" suburbs.

### 20th century to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 20th century to present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/Slums_in_milwaukee_1936.png/250px-Slums_in_milwaukee_1936.png)](/wiki/File:Slums_in_milwaukee_1936.png)

A [slum](/wiki/Slum "Slum") area of Milwaukee from 1936

During the first sixty years of the 20th century, Milwaukee was the major city in which the [Socialist Party of America](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") earned the highest votes. Milwaukee elected three [mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") who ran on the ticket of the Socialist Party: [Emil Seidel](/wiki/Emil_Seidel "Emil Seidel") (1910–1912), [Daniel Hoan](/wiki/Daniel_Hoan "Daniel Hoan") (1916–1940), and [Frank Zeidler](/wiki/Frank_Zeidler "Frank Zeidler") (1948–1960), with [Victor L. Berger](/wiki/Victor_L._Berger "Victor L. Berger") elected as the first socialist congressman in 1910. Often referred to as "[Sewer Socialists](/wiki/Sewer_Socialists "Sewer Socialists")", the Milwaukee Socialists were characterized by their practical approach to government and labor.[[56]](#cite_note-57) On October 14, 1912, former president [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), who ran for a third term under his progressive, [Bull Moose Party](/wiki/Bull_Moose_Party "Bull Moose Party"), survived an assassination attempt by salonkeeper [John Fleming Shrank](/wiki/Attempted_assassination_of_Theodore_Roosevelt "Attempted assassination of Theodore Roosevelt"), while making a speech at the [Gilpatrick Hotel](/wiki/Gilpatrick_Hotel "Gilpatrick Hotel"). On November 24, 1917, Milwaukee was the site of a [terrorist explosion](/wiki/Milwaukee_Police_Department_bombing "Milwaukee Police Department bombing") when a large black powder bomb[[57]](#cite_note-Balousek,_Marv_1997_p._113-58) exploded at the central police station at Oneida and Broadway.[[58]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-59) Nine members of the department were killed in the blast, along with a female civilian, Catherine Walker.[[57]](#cite_note-Balousek,_Marv_1997_p._113-58)[[59]](#cite_note-city.milwaukee.gov-60) It was suspected at the time that the bomb had been placed outside the church by anarchists, particularly the *[Galleanist](/wiki/Galleanist "Galleanist")* faction led by adherents of [Luigi Galleani](/wiki/Luigi_Galleani "Luigi Galleani"). At the time, the bombing was the most fatal single event in national law enforcement history.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

In the 1920s, [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") gangster activity came north to Milwaukee during the [Prohibition era](/wiki/Prohibition_era "Prohibition era"). [Al Capone](/wiki/Al_Capone "Al Capone"), noted Chicago mobster, owned a home in the Milwaukee suburb [Brookfield](/wiki/Brookfield,_Wisconsin "Brookfield, Wisconsin"), where [moonshine](/wiki/Moonshine "Moonshine") was made. The house still stands on a street named after Capone.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

By 1925, around 9,000 [Mexicans](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") lived in Milwaukee, but the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") forced many of them to move back south. In the 1950s, the Hispanic community was beginning to emerge. They arrived for jobs, filling positions in the manufacturing and agricultural sectors. During this time there were labor shortages due to the immigration laws that had reduced immigration from Eastern and Southern Europe. Additionally, strikes contributed to the labor shortages.[[62]](#cite_note-test-63)

In the mid-20th century, African Americans from Chicago moved to the North side of Milwaukee.[[63]](#cite_note-64) Milwaukee has attracted a population of Russians and other Eastern Europeans who began migrating in the early 1900s and the 1980s.[[64]](#cite_note-65) Many Hispanics of mostly Puerto Rican and Mexican heritage live on the south side of Milwaukee.[[65]](#cite_note-66) In the 1930s the city was severely segregated via [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining"). In 1960, African-American residents made up 15 percent of Milwaukee's population, yet the city was still among the most segregated of that time. As of 2019, at least three out of four black residents in Milwaukee would have to move to create racially integrated neighborhoods.[[13]](#cite_note-Leah_Foltman_&_Malia_Jones-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Milwaukee_October_2025_47_%28city_skyline_from_Lakeshore_State_Park%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_October_2025_47_%28city_skyline_from_Lakeshore_State_Park%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_October_2025_47_(city_skyline_from_Lakeshore_State_Park)_(cropped).jpg)

[Downtown Milwaukee](/wiki/Downtown_Milwaukee "Downtown Milwaukee") from Lakeshore State Park

Milwaukee's population peaked at 741,324 in 1960, where the Census Bureau reported the city's population as 91.1% white and 8.4% black.[[66]](#cite_note-67) By the late 1960s, Milwaukee's population had started to decline as people moved to suburbs, aided by ease of highways and offering the advantages of less crime, new housing, and lower taxation.[[67]](#cite_note-68) Milwaukee had a population of 594,833 by 2010, while the population of the overall metropolitan area increased. Given its large immigrant population and historic neighborhoods, Milwaukee avoided the severe declines of some of its fellow "[Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt")" cities.

Since the 1980s, the city has begun to make strides in improving its economy, neighborhoods, and image, resulting in the revitalization of neighborhoods such as the [Historic Third Ward](/wiki/Historic_Third_Ward "Historic Third Ward"), [Lincoln Village](/wiki/Lincoln_Village,_City_of_Milwaukee,_Wisconsin "Lincoln Village, City of Milwaukee, Wisconsin"), the [East Side](/wiki/East_Side,_Milwaukee "East Side, Milwaukee"), and more recently Walker's Point and [Bay View](/wiki/Bay_View,_Milwaukee "Bay View, Milwaukee"), along with attracting new businesses to its downtown area. These efforts have substantially slowed the population decline and have stabilized many parts of Milwaukee. Largely through its efforts to preserve its history, Milwaukee was named one of the "Dozen Distinctive Destinations" by the [National Trust for Historic Preservation](/wiki/National_Trust_for_Historic_Preservation "National Trust for Historic Preservation") in 2006.[[68]](#cite_note-distinctive-69) Historic Milwaukee walking tours provide a guided tour of Milwaukee's historic districts, including topics on Milwaukee's architectural heritage, its glass skywalk system, and the [Milwaukee Riverwalk](/wiki/Milwaukee_Riverwalk "Milwaukee Riverwalk").

In the 2010s and 2020s, Milwaukee underwent several development projects, including the opening of the [Bradley Symphony Center](/wiki/Bradley_Symphony_Center "Bradley Symphony Center").[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71) [The Hop streetcar system](/wiki/The_Hop_(streetcar) "The Hop (streetcar)") began operation in 2018, and the [Fiserv Forum](/wiki/Fiserv_Forum "Fiserv Forum") opened the same year.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73)

The city was hit by [widespread flash flooding on August 9–10, 2025](/wiki/2025_Milwaukee_flood "2025 Milwaukee flood"), after the second-most recorded rain over a two-day period in the city's history.[[73]](#cite_note-Why_did_Milwaukee_flood-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Milwaukee_05741u.jpg/960px-Milwaukee_05741u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_05741u.jpg)

Panorama map of Milwaukee, with a view of the [City Hall](/wiki/Milwaukee_City_Hall "Milwaukee City Hall") tower, c. 1898

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Milwaukee_Cityscape_by_Isaac_Rowlett_2025.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Cityscape_by_Isaac_Rowlett_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Cityscape_by_Isaac_Rowlett_2025.jpg)

Looking south from Milwaukee's Lower East Side, including Yankee Hill, Juneau Town, and downtown

Milwaukee lies along the shores and bluffs of [Lake Michigan](/wiki/Lake_Michigan "Lake Michigan") at the [confluence](/wiki/Confluence "Confluence") of three rivers: the [Menomonee](/wiki/Menomonee_River "Menomonee River"), the [Kinnickinnic](/wiki/Kinnickinnic_River_(Milwaukee_River) "Kinnickinnic River (Milwaukee River)"), and the [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River"). Smaller rivers, such as the [Root River](/wiki/Root_River_(Wisconsin) "Root River (Wisconsin)") and Lincoln Creek, also flow through the city.

Milwaukee's terrain is sculpted by the glacier path and includes steep bluffs along Lake Michigan that begin about a mile (1.6 km) north of downtown. In addition, 30 miles (48 km) west of Milwaukee is the Kettle Moraine and lake country that provides an industrial landscape combined with inland lakes.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 96.80 square miles (250.71 km2), of which, 96.12 square miles (248.95 km2) is land and 0.68 square miles (1.76 km2) is water.[[74]](#cite_note-Gazetteer_files-75) The city is overwhelmingly (99.89% of its area) in [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County "Milwaukee County"), but there are two tiny unpopulated portions that extend into neighboring counties.[[75]](#cite_note-foran-counties-76)[[A]](#cite_note-77)

### Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Milwaukee "List of tallest buildings in Milwaukee") and [Neighborhoods of Milwaukee](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_50_%28Historic_Third_Ward%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_50_%28Historic_Third_Ward%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_50_(Historic_Third_Ward).jpg)

The [Historic Third Ward](/wiki/Historic_Third_Ward_(Milwaukee) "Historic Third Ward (Milwaukee)") from the Milwaukee River

North–south streets are numbered, and east–west streets are named. However, north–south streets east of 1st Street are named, like east–west streets. The north–south numbering line is along the Menomonee River (east of Hawley Road) and Fairview Avenue/Golfview Parkway (west of Hawley Road), with the east–west numbering line defined along 1st Street (north of Oklahoma Avenue) and Chase/Howell Avenue (south of Oklahoma Avenue). This numbering system is also used to the north by [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon "Mequon") in [Ozaukee County](/wiki/Ozaukee_County "Ozaukee County"), and by some [Waukesha County](/wiki/Waukesha_County "Waukesha County") communities.

Milwaukee is crossed by [Interstate 43](/wiki/Interstate_43 "Interstate 43") and [Interstate 94](/wiki/Interstate_94 "Interstate 94"), which come together [downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Milwaukee "Downtown Milwaukee") at the [Marquette Interchange](/wiki/Marquette_Interchange "Marquette Interchange"). The [Interstate 894](/wiki/Interstate_894 "Interstate 894") bypass (which as of May 2015 also contains [Interstate 41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41")) runs through portions of the city's southwest side, and [Interstate 794](/wiki/Interstate_794 "Interstate 794") comes out of the Marquette interchange eastbound, bends south along the lakefront and crosses the harbor over the [Hoan Bridge](/wiki/Hoan_Bridge "Hoan Bridge"), then ends near the [Bay View](/wiki/Bay_View,_Milwaukee "Bay View, Milwaukee") [neighborhood](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee") and becomes the "Lake Parkway" ([WIS-794](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_794 "Wisconsin Highway 794")).

One of the distinctive traits of Milwaukee's residential areas are the neighborhoods full of so-called [Polish flats](/wiki/Polish_flat "Polish flat"). These are two-[family](/wiki/Family "Family") [homes](/wiki/Home "Home") with separate entrances, but with the units stacked one on top of another instead of side-by-side. This arrangement enables a family of limited means to purchase both a home and a modestly priced [rental](/wiki/Rental "Rental") [apartment](/wiki/Apartment "Apartment") unit. Since [Polish-American](/wiki/Polish-American "Polish-American") immigrants to the area prized land ownership, this solution, which was prominent in their areas of settlement within the city, came to be associated with them.[[76]](#cite_note-78)

The tallest building in the city is the [U.S. Bank Center](/wiki/U.S._Bank_Center_(Milwaukee) "U.S. Bank Center (Milwaukee)") in downtown Milwaukee, at 601 feet ( 183m ) tall, completed in 1973. In 2024 *[Architectural Digest](/wiki/Architectural_Digest "Architectural Digest")*, a prominent design publication, rated Milwaukee's skyline as the 15th most beautiful skyline in the world.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

See also: [Climate change in Wisconsin](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Wisconsin "Climate change in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Milwaukee_February_2025_26_%28Veterans_Park%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_February_2025_26_%28Veterans_Park%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_February_2025_26_(Veterans_Park).jpg)

Veterans Park in winter

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Milwaukee_November_2022_17_%28W._Wisconsin_Avenue_from_Milwaukee_Skywalk%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_November_2022_17_%28W._Wisconsin_Avenue_from_Milwaukee_Skywalk%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_November_2022_17_(W._Wisconsin_Avenue_from_Milwaukee_Skywalk).jpg)

West Wisconsin Avenue from the [Milwaukee Skywalk](/wiki/Milwaukee_Skywalk "Milwaukee Skywalk")

Milwaukee's location in the [Great Lakes Region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_Region "Great Lakes Region") often has rapidly changing weather, producing a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Dfa*), with cold, snowy winters, and hot, humid summers. The warmest month of the year is July, with a mean temperature of 73.3 °F (22.9 °C), while January is the coldest month, with a mean temperature of 24.0 °F (−4.4 °C).

Because of Milwaukee's proximity to Lake Michigan, a convection current forms around mid-afternoon in light wind, resulting in the so-called "lake breeze" – a smaller scale version of the more common [sea breeze](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze"). The lake breeze is most common between March and July. This onshore flow causes cooler temperatures to move inland usually 5 to 15 miles (8 to 24 km), with much warmer conditions persisting further inland. Because Milwaukee's official climate site, [Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mitchell_International_Airport "Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport"), is only 3 miles (4.8 km) from the lake, seasonal temperature variations are less extreme than in many other locations of the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area").

As the sun sets, the convection current reverses and an offshore flow ensues causing a land breeze. After a land breeze develops, warmer temperatures flow east toward the lakeshore, sometimes causing high temperatures during the late evening. The lake breeze is not a daily occurrence and will not usually form if a southwest, west, or northwest wind generally exceeds 15 mph (24 km/h). The lake moderates cold air outbreaks along the lakeshore during winter months.

Aside from the lake's influence, overnight lows in downtown Milwaukee year-round are often much warmer than suburban locations because of the [urban heat island effect](/wiki/Urban_heat_island_effect "Urban heat island effect"). Onshore winds elevate daytime [relative humidity](/wiki/Relative_humidity "Relative humidity") levels in Milwaukee as compared to inland locations nearby.

Thunderstorms in the region can be dangerous and damaging, bringing [hail](/wiki/Hail "Hail") and high winds. In rare instances, they can bring a [tornado](/wiki/Tornado "Tornado"). However, almost all summer rainfall in the city is brought by these storms. In spring and fall, longer events of prolonged, lighter rain bring most of the [precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation "Precipitation"). A moderate snow cover can be seen on or linger for many winter days, but even during meteorological winter, on average, over 40% of days see less than 1 inch (2.5 cm) on the ground.[[78]](#cite_note-NOAA_txt-80)

Milwaukee tends to experience highs that are 90 °F (32 °C) or above on about nine days per year, and lows at or below 0 °F (−18 °C) on six to seven nights.[[78]](#cite_note-NOAA_txt-80) Extremes range from 105 °F (41 °C) set on July 24, 1934, down to −26 °F (−32 °C) on both January 17, 1982, and February 4, 1996.[[79]](#cite_note-NOAA-81) The 1982 event, also known as [Cold Sunday](/wiki/Cold_Sunday "Cold Sunday"), featured temperatures as low as −40 °F (−40 °C) in some of the [suburbs](/wiki/Suburb "Suburb") as little as 10 miles (16 km) to the north of Milwaukee.

#### Climate change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Climate change")]

According to the United States' [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency"), Milwaukee is threatened by ongoing [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") which is warming the planet. These risks include worsened heat waves because many of its residents do not possess [air conditioners](/wiki/Air_conditioner "Air conditioner"), concerns about the water quality of Lake Michigan, and increased chances of flooding from intense rainstorms.[[83]](#cite_note-EPA-87) In 2018, Milwaukee mayor [Tom Barrett](/wiki/Tom_Barrett_(Wisconsin_politician) "Tom Barrett (Wisconsin politician)") announced that the city would uphold its obligations under the [Paris Agreement](/wiki/Paris_Agreement "Paris Agreement"), despite the United States' withdrawal, and set a goal moving a quarter of the city's electricity sources to renewable energy by 2025. These have included expansions in the city's solar power-generating capacity and a [wind turbine](/wiki/Wind_turbine "Wind turbine")'s installation near the Port of Milwaukee. Other actions being taken include local incentives for energy-saving upgrades to homes and businesses.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Water")]

In the 1990s and 2000s, Lake Michigan experienced large [algae blooms](/wiki/Algae_blooms "Algae blooms"), which can threaten aquatic life. Responding to this problem, in 2009 the city became an "Innovating City" in the [Global Compact](/wiki/United_Nations_Global_Compact "United Nations Global Compact") Cities Program. The Milwaukee Water Council was also formed in 2009.[[85]](#cite_note-89) Its objectives were to "better understand the processes related to freshwater systems dynamics" and to develop "a policy and management program aimed at balancing the protection and utilization of freshwater". The strategy used the [Circles of Sustainability](/wiki/Circles_of_Sustainability "Circles of Sustainability") method. Instead of treating the water quality problem as a single environmental issue, the Water Council draws on the Circles method to analyze the interconnection among ecological, economic, political and cultural factors.[[86]](#cite_note-90) This holistic water treatment helped Milwaukee win the US Water Alliance's 2012 US Water Prize.[[87]](#cite_note-91) In 2009 the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee also established the [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee School of Freshwater Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee_School_of_Freshwater_Sciences "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee School of Freshwater Sciences"), the first graduate school of [limnology](/wiki/Limnology "Limnology") in the United States.

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit), there are more than 3,000 drinking fountains in the Milwaukee Public School District; 183 had lead levels above 15 parts per billion (ppb). 15 ppb is the federal action level in which effort needs to be taken to lower these lead levels.[[88]](#cite_note-92) In the city, more than 10% of children test positive for dangerous lead levels in their blood as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit).[[89]](#cite_note-93)

Milwaukee is the [31st-most populous](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population") city in the United States, and anchors the [39th-most populous metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the US.[[91]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2019-95)[[92]](#cite_note-96)[[93]](#cite_note-97)[[94]](#cite_note-98) Its [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") population makes it the 29th-most populous in the US. The city's population has dropped at every census count since 1970. In 2012, Milwaukee was listed as a gamma city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").

### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

In the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[95]](#cite_note-2020-census-5553000-99) the population was 577,222. The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 6,001.2 inhabitants per square mile (2,317.1/km2). There were 257,723 housing units at an average density of 2,679.5 per square mile (1,034.6/km2). Ethnically, the population was 20.1% [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") of any race. When grouping both Hispanic and non-Hispanic people together by race, the city was 38.6% [Black](/wiki/Black_(U.S._Census) "Black (U.S. Census)") or [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._census) "African American (U.S. census)"), 36.1% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 5.2% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.9% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 9.0% from [other races](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_the_United_States_census "Race and ethnicity in the United States census"), and 10.1% from two or more races.

The 2020 census population of the city included 1,198 people incarcerated in adult correctional facilities and 9,625 people in university student housing.[[96]](#cite_note-2020-P5-5553000-100)

In [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimates for 2016–2020, the median household income was $43,125, and the median family income was $51,170. Male full-time worker median income was $42,859, versus $37,890 for female workers. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $24,167. About 19.6% of families and 24.6% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_line "Poverty line"), including 35.1% of those under age 18 and 14.5% of those age 65 or over.[[97]](#cite_note-2020-EconChar-5553000-101) Of the population age 25 and over, 84.4% were high school graduates or higher and 24.6% had a bachelor's degree or higher.[[98]](#cite_note-2020-SocChar-5553000-102)
