In the 2006–2008 American Community Survey, 38.3% of Milwaukee's residents reported having [African American](/wiki/African_American "African American") ancestry and 20.8% reported [German](/wiki/German_American "German American") ancestry. Other significant population groups include [Polish](/wiki/Polish_American "Polish American") (8.8%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_American "Irish American") (6.5%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_American "Italian American") (3.6%), [English](/wiki/English_American "English American") (2.8%), and [French](/wiki/French_American "French American") (1.7%). According to the 2010 United States census, the largest Hispanic backgrounds in Milwaukee as of 2010 were: Mexican (69,680), Puerto Rican (24,672), Other Hispanic or Latino (3,808), Central American (1,962), South American (1,299), Cuban (866) and Dominican (720).[[101]](#cite_note-107) Per the 2022 American Community Survey five-year estimates, the [German American](/wiki/German_American "German American") population was 87,601.[[102]](#cite_note-108) Per the 2023 American Community Survey one-year estimates, the [Mexican American](/wiki/Mexican_American "Mexican American") population was 82,845, comprising over 60% of the city's Latino population.[[103]](#cite_note-109)

in 2002, the [Milwaukee metropolitan area](/wiki/Milwaukee_metropolitan_area "Milwaukee metropolitan area") was cited as being the most segregated in the U.S. in a *[Jet Magazine](/wiki/Jet_Magazine "Jet Magazine")* article.[[104]](#cite_note-hypersegregation-110) The source of this information was a segregation index developed in the mid-1950s and used since 1964. In 2003, a non-peer-reviewed study was conducted by hired researchers at the [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee") which claimed Milwaukee is not "hypersegregated" and instead ranks as the 43rd most integrated city in America.[[105]](#cite_note-111) According to research by demographer [William H. Frey](/wiki/William_H._Frey "William H. Frey") using the [index of dissimilarity](/wiki/Index_of_dissimilarity "Index of dissimilarity") method and data from the [2010 United States census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), Milwaukee has the highest level of black-white segregation of any of the 100 largest metropolitan areas in the United States.[[106]](#cite_note-112) Through continued dialogue between Milwaukee's citizens, the city is trying to reduce racial tensions and the rate of segregation.[[107]](#cite_note-segregation-113) With demographic changes in the wake of [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight"), segregation in metropolitan Milwaukee is primarily in the suburbs rather than the city, as in the era of [Father Groppi](/wiki/Father_Groppi "Father Groppi").[[108]](#cite_note-integration-114)[[109]](#cite_note-QuinnCensus-115)

In 2015, Milwaukee was rated as the "worst city for black Americans" based on disparities in employment and income levels.[[110]](#cite_note-116) The city's black population experiences high levels of [incarceration](/wiki/Incarceration_in_the_United_States "Incarceration in the United States") and a severe [educational achievement gap](/wiki/Achievement_gap_in_the_United_States "Achievement gap in the United States").[[111]](#cite_note-117)

Per the 2022 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") five-year estimates, the [Hmong American](/wiki/Hmong_American "Hmong American") population was 11,469,[[112]](#cite_note-118) numerically the largest Hmong population of any city in Wisconsin. In 2013, Mark Pfeifer, the editor of the *[Hmong Studies Journal](/wiki/Hmong_Studies_Journal "Hmong Studies Journal")*, stated [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_people "Hmong people") in Milwaukee had recently been moving to the northwest side of Milwaukee; they historically lived in the north and south areas of Milwaukee.[[113]](#cite_note-Pabst-119) The [Hmong American Peace Academy](/wiki/Hmong_American_Peace_Academy "Hmong American Peace Academy")/[International Peace Academy](/wiki/International_Peace_Academy_(Milwaukee) "International Peace Academy (Milwaukee)"), a K–12 school system in Milwaukee centered on the [Hmong community](/wiki/Hmong_in_Wisconsin "Hmong in Wisconsin"), opened in 2004.[[113]](#cite_note-Pabst-119)

Polish people, Slavs, European Jews, people from the Mediterranean including Greeks, Italians, and Syrians immigrated to Milwaukee after 1880.[[114]](#cite_note-120)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Religion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Milwaukee_Cathedral_3.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Cathedral_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Cathedral_3.jpg)

The [Basilica of St. Josaphat](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._Josaphat "Basilica of St. Josaphat") in Milwaukee's historic [Lincoln Village](/wiki/Lincoln_Village,_Milwaukee "Lincoln Village, Milwaukee")

In 2010, approximately 51.8% of residents in the Milwaukee area said they regularly attended religious services. In 2012, 24.6% of the Milwaukee area population identified as [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic"), 10.8% as [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"), 1.6% as [Methodist](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist"), and 0.6% as [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism").[[115]](#cite_note-121) The Milwaukee metro area contains the majority of the state's Jewish population,[[116]](#cite_note-Sheskin-122) and has a long [history of Jewish immigration](/wiki/Jews_in_Milwaukee "Jews in Milwaukee") from German-speaking and Eastern European countries.[[117]](#cite_note-Cohen-123)

The [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milwaukee "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee") is headquartered in the city. The [School Sisters of St. Francis](/wiki/School_Sisters_of_St._Francis "School Sisters of St. Francis") have their [motherhouse](/wiki/Motherhouse "Motherhouse") in Milwaukee, and several other religious orders have a significant presence in the area, including the [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") and [Franciscans](/wiki/Franciscan "Franciscan"). Milwaukee, where [Joseph Kentenich](/wiki/Joseph_Kentenich "Joseph Kentenich") was exiled from 1952 to 1965, is the center for the [Schoenstatt Movement](/wiki/Schoenstatt_Movement "Schoenstatt Movement") in the US.

Milwaukee is home to numerous historic Catholic parishes, including the [Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._John_the_Evangelist_(Milwaukee) "Cathedral of St. John the Evangelist (Milwaukee)"). The oldest church building in Milwaukee is [St. Joan of Arc Chapel](/wiki/St._Joan_of_Arc_Chapel "St. Joan of Arc Chapel"), which was built c. 1420 in France and presently located on the [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University") campus. The [Basilica of St. Josaphat](/wiki/Basilica_of_St._Josaphat "Basilica of St. Josaphat") was the first church to be given [basicila designation](/wiki/Basilicas_in_the_Catholic_Church "Basilicas in the Catholic Church") in Wisconsin and the third in the US. [Holy Hill National Shrine of Mary, Help of Christians](/wiki/Holy_Hill_National_Shrine_of_Mary,_Help_of_Christians "Holy Hill National Shrine of Mary, Help of Christians"), northwest of Milwaukee in [Hubertus, Wisconsin](/wiki/Hubertus,_Wisconsin "Hubertus, Wisconsin"), was made a basilica in 2006.

The [Episcopal Diocese of Milwaukee](/wiki/Episcopal_Diocese_of_Milwaukee "Episcopal Diocese of Milwaukee") is based in the city, as are several [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") bodies, including the [Greater Milwaukee Synod](/wiki/Greater_Milwaukee_Synod "Greater Milwaukee Synod") of the [Evangelical Lutheran Church in America](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_in_America "Evangelical Lutheran Church in America"); the [South Wisconsin District](/wiki/South_Wisconsin_District_of_the_Lutheran_Church_%E2%80%93_Missouri_Synod "South Wisconsin District of the Lutheran Church – Missouri Synod") of the [Lutheran Church – Missouri Synod](/wiki/Lutheran_Church_%E2%80%93_Missouri_Synod "Lutheran Church – Missouri Synod"), which operates [Concordia University Wisconsin](/wiki/Concordia_University_Wisconsin "Concordia University Wisconsin") in the suburb of [Mequon](/wiki/Mequon,_Wisconsin "Mequon, Wisconsin"); and the [Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod](/wiki/Wisconsin_Evangelical_Lutheran_Synod "Wisconsin Evangelical Lutheran Synod"), which was founded in Milwaukee in 1850 and has headquarters in the suburb of [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha,_Wisconsin "Waukesha, Wisconsin"). [Milwaukee Lutheran High School](/wiki/Milwaukee_Lutheran_High_School "Milwaukee Lutheran High School") and [Wisconsin Lutheran High School](/wiki/Wisconsin_Lutheran_High_School "Wisconsin Lutheran High School") are the nation's oldest Lutheran high schools.

The [St. Sava Serbian Orthodox Cathedral](/wiki/St._Sava_Serbian_Orthodox_Cathedral_(Milwaukee) "St. Sava Serbian Orthodox Cathedral (Milwaukee)") is a landmark of the Serbian community in Milwaukee, located by the American Serb hall, which the congregation also operated until putting it up for sale in January 2021 due to financial challenges caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[118]](#cite_note-124)

[The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") has a presence in the Milwaukee area. The Milwaukee area has two stakes, with fourteen wards and four branches among them. The closest temple is the [Chicago Illinois Temple](/wiki/Chicago_Illinois_Temple "Chicago Illinois Temple"). The area is part of the [Wisconsin Milwaukee Mission](/wiki/Mission_(LDS_Church) "Mission (LDS Church)").[[119]](#cite_note-LDS-125)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Harley-Davidson_Motorcycle_Factory_Building_original_site.jpg/250px-Harley-Davidson_Motorcycle_Factory_Building_original_site.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harley-Davidson_Motorcycle_Factory_Building_original_site.jpg)

Motorcycle manufacturer [Harley-Davidson](/wiki/Harley-Davidson "Harley-Davidson") is based in Milwaukee; the [Harley-Davidson Museum](/wiki/Harley-Davidson_Museum "Harley-Davidson Museum") chronicles its history.

Milwaukee is rated as a "Sufficiency" city by the [Globalization and World Cities Research Network](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network").[[120]](#cite_note-126) The city is home to the international headquarters of five [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") companies: [Northwestern Mutual](/wiki/Northwestern_Mutual "Northwestern Mutual"), [Fiserv](/wiki/Fiserv "Fiserv"), [ManpowerGroup](/wiki/ManpowerGroup "ManpowerGroup"), [Rockwell Automation](/wiki/Rockwell_Automation "Rockwell Automation"), and [WEC Energy Group](/wiki/WEC_Energy_Group "WEC Energy Group").[[15]](#cite_note-fortune-16) Other companies based in Milwaukee include [Harley-Davidson](/wiki/Harley-Davidson "Harley-Davidson"), [Briggs & Stratton](/wiki/Briggs_%26_Stratton "Briggs & Stratton"), [Brady Corporation](/wiki/Brady_Corporation "Brady Corporation"), [Baird (investment bank)](/wiki/Baird_(investment_bank) "Baird (investment bank)"), [Alliance Federated Energy](/wiki/Alliance_Federated_Energy "Alliance Federated Energy"), [Sensient Technologies](/wiki/Sensient_Technologies "Sensient Technologies"), [Hal Leonard](/wiki/Hal_Leonard "Hal Leonard"), [Direct Supply](/wiki/Direct_Supply "Direct Supply"), [Rite-Hite](/wiki/Rite-Hite "Rite-Hite"), the [American Society for Quality](/wiki/American_Society_for_Quality "American Society for Quality"), [A. O. Smith](/wiki/A._O._Smith "A. O. Smith"), [Rexnord](/wiki/Rexnord "Rexnord"), [Master Lock](/wiki/Master_Lock "Master Lock"), [Marcus Corporation](/wiki/Marcus_Corporation "Marcus Corporation"), [REV Group](/wiki/REV_Group "REV Group"), [American Signal Corporation](/wiki/American_Signal_Corporation "American Signal Corporation"),[[121]](#cite_note-127) [GE Healthcare](/wiki/GE_Healthcare "GE Healthcare"), Diagnostic Imaging and Clinical Systems, and [MGIC Investments](/wiki/MGIC_Investment_Corporation "MGIC Investment Corporation").

The Milwaukee metropolitan area ranks fifth in the United States in terms of the number of *Fortune* 500 company headquarters as a share of the population. Milwaukee also has a large number of financial service firms, particularly those specializing in mutual funds and transaction processing systems, and a number of publishing and printing companies. Service and managerial jobs are the fastest-growing segments of the Milwaukee economy, and [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") alone makes up 27% of the jobs in the city.[[122]](#cite_note-128)

### Brewing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Brewing")]

Further information: [Beer in Milwaukee](/wiki/Beer_in_Milwaukee "Beer in Milwaukee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Miller_Brewery.png/250px-Miller_Brewery.png)](/wiki/File:Miller_Brewery.png)

[Miller Brewing Company](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company"), founded in 1855, has its main brewery in Milwaukee's West Side.

Milwaukee became synonymous with [Germans](/wiki/Germans "Germans") and [beer](/wiki/Beer "Beer") beginning in the 1840s. The Germans had long enjoyed beer and set up breweries when they arrived in Milwaukee. By 1856, there were more than two dozen breweries in Milwaukee, most of them owned and operated by Germans. Besides making beer for the rest of the nation, Milwaukeeans enjoyed consuming the various beers produced in the city's breweries. As early as 1843, pioneer historian James Buck recorded 138 taverns in Milwaukee, an average of one per forty residents. Today, [beer halls](/wiki/Beer_hall "Beer hall") and taverns are abundant in the city, but only one of the major breweries—[Miller](/wiki/Miller_Brewing_Company "Miller Brewing Company")—remains in Milwaukee.[[123]](#cite_note-milwaukeehistory.net-129)

Milwaukee was once the home to four of the world's largest beer breweries ([Schlitz](/wiki/Joseph_Schlitz_Brewing_Company "Joseph Schlitz Brewing Company"), [Blatz](/wiki/Valentin_Blatz_Brewing_Company "Valentin Blatz Brewing Company"), [Pabst](/wiki/Pabst_Brewing_Company "Pabst Brewing Company"), and Miller), and was the number one beer producing city in the world for many years, giving it the nickname " Brew City ". As late as 1981, Milwaukee had the greatest brewing capacity in the world.[[124]](#cite_note-Breweries_Shut_Off_Tap-130) Despite the decline in its position as the world's leading beer producer after the loss of two of those breweries, Miller Brewing Company remains a key employer by employing over 2,200 of the city's workers.[[125]](#cite_note-131) Because of Miller's position as the second-largest beer-maker in the U.S., the city remains known as a beer town. The city and surrounding areas are seeing a resurgence in microbreweries, nanobreweries and brewpubs with the craft beer movement.[[126]](#cite_note-132)

The historic Milwaukee Brewery in "Miller Valley" at 4000 West State Street, is the oldest functioning major brewery in the United States. In addition to Miller and the heavily automated [Leinenkugel's](/wiki/Leinenkugel%27s "Leinenkugel's") brewery in the old Blatz 10th Street plant, other stand-alone breweries in Milwaukee include [Milwaukee Brewing Company](/wiki/Milwaukee_Ale_House "Milwaukee Ale House"), a microbrewery in [Walker's Point neighborhood](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_Milwaukee#Walker's_Point "Neighborhoods of Milwaukee"); [Lakefront Brewery](/wiki/Lakefront_Brewery "Lakefront Brewery"), a [microbrewery](/wiki/Microbrewery "Microbrewery") in Brewers Hill; and [Sprecher Brewery](/wiki/Sprecher_Brewery "Sprecher Brewery"), a German brewery that also brews craft sodas. Since 2015, nearly two dozen craft brewing companies have been established in the city.[[127]](#cite_note-Late-133)[[128]](#cite_note-22_New-134)

### Other industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Other industries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Mounds_of_coal%2C_catwalks%2C_and_barges_at_the_Milwaukee_Western_Fuel_Company_coal_docks%2C_12-1942_-_NARA_-_535923.jpg/250px-Mounds_of_coal%2C_catwalks%2C_and_barges_at_the_Milwaukee_Western_Fuel_Company_coal_docks%2C_12-1942_-_NARA_-_535923.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mounds_of_coal,_catwalks,_and_barges_at_the_Milwaukee_Western_Fuel_Company_coal_docks,_12-1942_-_NARA_-_535923.jpg)

Milwaukee Western Fuel Company coal docks in the [Menomonee Valley](/wiki/Menomonee_Valley "Menomonee Valley"), 1942

Milwaukee was situated as a port city and a center for collecting and distributing produce. Some of the new [immigrants](/wiki/Immigrants "Immigrants") who were settling into the new state of Wisconsin during the middle of the 19th century were wheat farmers. By 1860, Wisconsin was one of the major producers of wheat. [Rail transport](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") was needed to transport this grain from the wheat fields of Wisconsin to Milwaukee's harbor. Improvements in railways at the time made this possible.[[129]](#cite_note-135)

There was intense competition for markets with [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), situated across the state line in [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), and, to a lesser degree, with [Racine](/wiki/Racine,_Wisconsin "Racine, Wisconsin") and [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha "Kenosha") in Wisconsin. Eventually, Chicago won out due to its superior financial markets and transportation position, including the [Chicago Portage](/wiki/Chicago_Portage "Chicago Portage") and being the hub of the railroad lines in the United States. Milwaukee did solidify its place as the commercial capital of Wisconsin and an important market in the Midwest.[[123]](#cite_note-milwaukeehistory.net-129)

Because of its easy access to Lake Michigan and other waterways, Milwaukee's [Menomonee Valley](/wiki/Menomonee_Valley "Menomonee Valley") has historically been home to [manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing "Manufacturing"), [stockyards](/wiki/Meat_packing_industry "Meat packing industry"), [rendering plants](/wiki/Rendering_(food_processing) "Rendering (food processing)"), [shipping](/wiki/Shipping "Shipping"), and other [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry").[[130]](#cite_note-136) Manufacturing was concentrated on the north side, with a peak of over 50 manufacturers in that industrialized area.[[131]](#cite_note-master-137)

Reshaping of the valley began with the [railroads](/wiki/Railroads "Railroads") built by city co-founder [Byron Kilbourn](/wiki/Byron_Kilbourn "Byron Kilbourn") to bring product from Wisconsin's farm interior to the port. By 1862 Milwaukee was the largest shipper of [wheat](/wiki/Wheat "Wheat") on the planet, and related industry developed. [Grain elevators](/wiki/Grain_elevator "Grain elevator") were built and, due to Milwaukee's dominant [German immigrant population](/wiki/German_American "German American"), [breweries](/wiki/Breweries "Breweries") sprang up around the processing of [barley](/wiki/Barley "Barley") and [hops](/wiki/Hops "Hops"). A number of [tanneries](/wiki/Tanneries "Tanneries") were constructed, of which the [Pfister & Vogel](/wiki/Pfister_%26_Vogel "Pfister & Vogel") tannery grew to become the largest in America.

In 1843 George Burnham and his brother Jonathan opened a [brickyard](/wiki/Brickyard "Brickyard") near 16th Street. When a durable and distinct cream-colored brick came out of the clay beds, other brickyards sprang up to take advantage of this resource. Because many of the city's buildings were built using this material it earned the nickname "Cream City", and consequently the brick was called [Cream City brick](/wiki/Cream_City_brick "Cream City brick"). By 1881 the Burnham brickyard, which employed 200 men and peaked at 15 million bricks a year, was the largest in the world.

[Flour mills](/wiki/Flour_mills "Flour mills"), packing plants, breweries, railways and tanneries further industrialized the valley. With the [marshlands](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh") drained and the [Kinnickinnic](/wiki/Kinnickinnic_River_(Milwaukee_River) "Kinnickinnic River (Milwaukee River)") and [Milwaukee Rivers](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") dredged, attention turned to the valley. Along with the processing industries, bulk commodity storage, machining, and manufacturing entered the scene. The valley was home to the [Milwaukee Road](/wiki/Milwaukee_Road "Milwaukee Road"), [Falk Corporation](/w/index.php?title=Falk_Corporation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Falk Corporation (page does not exist)"), [Cutler-Hammer](/wiki/Cutler-Hammer "Cutler-Hammer"), [Harnischfeger Corporation](/wiki/Harnischfeger_Corporation "Harnischfeger Corporation"), [Chain Belt Company](/wiki/Chain_Belt_Company "Chain Belt Company"), [Nordberg Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Nordberg_Manufacturing_Company "Nordberg Manufacturing Company") and other industry giants. Early in the 20th century, Milwaukee was home to several pioneer [brass era](/wiki/Brass_era "Brass era") [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") makers, including Ogren (1919–1922).[[132]](#cite_note-138)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Culture")]

Milwaukee is a popular location for sailing, boating, and kayaking on Lake Michigan, ethnic dining, and cultural festivals. Often referred to as the City of Festivals,[[133]](#cite_note-festivals-139) Milwaukee has various cultural events which take place throughout the summer at [Henry Maier Festival Park](/wiki/Henry_Maier_Festival_Park "Henry Maier Festival Park"), on the lake. Museums and cultural events, such as Jazz in the Park, occur weekly in downtown parks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Milwaukee_Art_Museum_Quadracci_Pavilion_2016.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Art_Museum_Quadracci_Pavilion_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Art_Museum_Quadracci_Pavilion_2016.jpg)

[Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum")

The [Milwaukee Art Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Art_Museum "Milwaukee Art Museum") is perhaps Milwaukee's most visually prominent cultural attraction, especially its $100 million wing designed by [Santiago Calatrava](/wiki/Santiago_Calatrava "Santiago Calatrava") in his first American commission.[[134]](#cite_note-Museum_Info-140) The museum includes a *brise soleil*, a moving sunscreen that unfolds similarly to the wing of a bird. The [Grohmann Museum](/wiki/Grohmann_Museum "Grohmann Museum") at the [Milwaukee School of Engineering](/wiki/Milwaukee_School_of_Engineering "Milwaukee School of Engineering") contains the world's most comprehensive art collection dedicated to the evolution of human work.[[135]](#cite_note-141) [Haggerty Museum of Art](/wiki/Haggerty_Museum_of_Art "Haggerty Museum of Art") on the [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University") campus houses several classical masterpieces and is open to the public. The [Villa Terrace Decorative Arts Museum](/wiki/Villa_Terrace_Decorative_Arts_Museum "Villa Terrace Decorative Arts Museum") is the former home of Lloyd Smith, president of the [A.O. Smith](/wiki/A.O._Smith "A.O. Smith") corporation, and has a terraced garden, an assortment of Renaissance art, and rotating exhibits.[[136]](#cite_note-142) [Charles Allis Art Museum](/wiki/Charles_Allis_Art_Museum "Charles Allis Art Museum"), in the Tudor-style mansion of Charles Allis, hosts several changing exhibits every year in the building's original antique furnished setting.

The [Milwaukee Public Museum](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Museum "Milwaukee Public Museum") has been Milwaukee's primary [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") and [human history](/wiki/History "History") museum for 125 years, with over 150,000 square feet (14,000 m2) of permanent exhibits.[[137]](#cite_note-143) Exhibits feature Africa, Europe, the Arctic, Oceania, and South and Middle America, ancient Western civilizations, dinosaurs, the tropical rainforest, streets of Old Milwaukee, a European Village, live insects and arthropods, a [Samson gorilla](/wiki/Samson_(gorilla) "Samson (gorilla)") replica, the Puelicher Butterfly Wing, hands-on laboratories, and animatronics. The museum also contains an [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") movie theater/planetarium. Milwaukee Public Museum owns the world's largest dinosaur skull.[[138]](#cite_note-144)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Milwaukee_June_2022_05_%28Discovery_World%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_June_2022_05_%28Discovery_World%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_June_2022_05_(Discovery_World).jpg)

[Discovery World](/wiki/Discovery_World_(museum) "Discovery World (museum)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Milwaukee%2C_pabst_mansion_01.jpg/250px-Milwaukee%2C_pabst_mansion_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee,_pabst_mansion_01.jpg)

[Pabst Mansion](/wiki/Pabst_Mansion "Pabst Mansion")

[Discovery World](/wiki/Discovery_World_(museum) "Discovery World (museum)"), Milwaukee's largest museum dedicated to science, is just south of the Milwaukee Art Museum along the lakefront. Visitors are drawn by its interactive exhibits, saltwater and freshwater aquariums, as well as touch tanks and digital theaters. A [double helix staircase](/wiki/Double_helix_staircase "Double helix staircase") wraps around the 40-foot (12 m) kinetic sculpture of a human genome. [Betty Brinn Children's Museum](/wiki/Betty_Brinn_Children%27s_Museum "Betty Brinn Children's Museum")[[139]](#cite_note-145) is geared toward children under ten years of age and is filled with hands-on exhibits and interactive programs, offering families a chance to learn together. Voted one of the top ten museums for children by [Parents Magazine](/wiki/Parents_Magazine "Parents Magazine"), it exemplifies the philosophy that constructive play nurtures the mind.

[Pabst Mansion](/wiki/Pabst_Mansion "Pabst Mansion") was built in 1892 by beer tycoon [Frederick Pabst](/wiki/Frederick_Pabst "Frederick Pabst") and was once considered the jewel of Milwaukee's famous avenue of mansions called the "Grand Avenue". Interior rooms have been restored with period furniture, to create an authentic replica of a Victorian Mansion. The [Milwaukee County Historical Society](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Historical_Society "Milwaukee County Historical Society") features Milwaukee during the late 19th century through the mid-20th century, including a research library. The Wisconsin Black Historical Society documents and preserves the historical heritage of African descent in Wisconsin, exhibiting collecting and disseminating materials depicting this heritage.[[140]](#cite_note-146) [America's Black Holocaust Museum](/wiki/America%27s_Black_Holocaust_Museum "America's Black Holocaust Museum"), founded by [lynching](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") survivor [James Cameron](/wiki/James_Cameron_(civil-rights_activist) "James Cameron (civil-rights activist)"), featured exhibits which chronicle the injustices suffered throughout history by African Americans in the United States.[[141]](#cite_note-147)[[142]](#cite_note-148)[[143]](#cite_note-149) The [Jewish Museum Milwaukee](/wiki/Jewish_Museum_Milwaukee "Jewish Museum Milwaukee") is dedicated to preserving and presenting the history of the Jewish people in southeastern Wisconsin and celebrating the continuum of Jewish heritage and culture.[[144]](#cite_note-150)

The [Harley-Davidson Museum](/wiki/Harley-Davidson_Museum "Harley-Davidson Museum"), opened in 2008, pays tribute to [Harley-Davidson](/wiki/Harley-Davidson "Harley-Davidson") motorcycles. The [Mitchell Gallery of Flight](/wiki/Mitchell_Gallery_of_Flight "Mitchell Gallery of Flight") at [Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mitchell_International_Airport "Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport") exhibits Milwaukee's [aviation](/wiki/Aviation "Aviation") history.

### Performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Performing arts")]

Milwaukee is home to a large performing arts scene, with numerous groups and venues showcasing a variety of performances. The [Bel Canto Chorus](/wiki/Bel_Canto_Chorus "Bel Canto Chorus") and [Florentine Opera](/wiki/Florentine_Opera "Florentine Opera") contribute to the city's choral and operatic traditions. The [Milwaukee Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Milwaukee_Symphony_Orchestra "Milwaukee Symphony Orchestra") and [Milwaukee Ballet](/wiki/Milwaukee_Ballet "Milwaukee Ballet") represent classic performing arts in the city. The [Milwaukee Repertory Theater](/wiki/Milwaukee_Repertory_Theater "Milwaukee Repertory Theater"), [Milwaukee Opera Theatre](/wiki/Milwaukee_Opera_Theatre "Milwaukee Opera Theatre"), and [Milwaukee Public Theatre](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Theatre "Milwaukee Public Theatre") each provide diverse theatrical experiences, from classic plays to contemporary works. [First Stage Children's Theater](/wiki/First_Stage_Children%27s_Theater "First Stage Children's Theater"), the [Milwaukee Youth Theatre](/wiki/Milwaukee_Youth_Theatre "Milwaukee Youth Theatre"), and [Milwaukee Youth Arts Center](/wiki/Milwaukee_Youth_Arts_Center "Milwaukee Youth Arts Center") provide children's theater entertainment. For live music, venues like [The Rave](/wiki/The_Rave "The Rave")/Eagles Ballroom, [Riverside Theater](/wiki/Riverside_Theater_(Milwaukee) "Riverside Theater (Milwaukee)"), [Turner Hall](/wiki/Turner_Hall_(Milwaukee) "Turner Hall (Milwaukee)"), and the [Pabst Theater](/wiki/Pabst_Theater "Pabst Theater") host concerts across a range of genres, while [Present Music](/wiki/Present_Music "Present Music") offers contemporary performances. Other notable venues include the [Miller High Life Theatre](/wiki/Miller_High_Life_Theatre "Miller High Life Theatre"), [Skylight Music Theatre](/wiki/Skylight_Music_Theatre "Skylight Music Theatre"), and the [Wisconsin Conservatory of Music](/wiki/Wisconsin_Conservatory_of_Music "Wisconsin Conservatory of Music").

### Public art and monuments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Public art and monuments")]

Main article: [List of public art in Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_public_art_in_Milwaukee "List of public art in Milwaukee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Black_Cat_Alley.jpg/250px-Black_Cat_Alley.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Cat_Alley.jpg)

[Black Cat Alley](/wiki/Black_Cat_Alley "Black Cat Alley")

Milwaukee has some 75 sculptures to honor the many people and topics reflecting the [city's history](/wiki/History_of_Milwaukee "History of Milwaukee").[[145]](#cite_note-151) Among the more prominent monuments are:

* *[The Calling](/wiki/The_Calling_(di_Suvero) "The Calling (di Suvero)")* I-beams
* [Friedrich Wilhelm von Steuben](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_von_Steuben "Friedrich Wilhelm von Steuben")
* [Tadeusz Kościuszko](/wiki/Tadeusz_Ko%C5%9Bciuszko "Tadeusz Kościuszko")
* [Casimir Pulaski](/wiki/Count_Casimir_Pulaski_(Kiselewski) "Count Casimir Pulaski (Kiselewski)")
* [Solomon Juneau](/wiki/Juneau_Monument "Juneau Monument")
* [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Statue_of_Abraham_Lincoln_(Milwaukee) "Statue of Abraham Lincoln (Milwaukee)")
* [George Washington](/wiki/Washington_Monument_(Milwaukee) "Washington Monument (Milwaukee)")
* [Bronze Fonz](/wiki/Bronze_Fonz "Bronze Fonz")
* [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II")
* [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.")
* *[The Victorious Charge](/wiki/The_Victorious_Charge "The Victorious Charge")*
* [Leif Ericson](/wiki/Leif,_the_Discoverer_(Whitney) "Leif, the Discoverer (Whitney)")
* [Jacques Marquette](/wiki/Jacques_Marquette "Jacques Marquette")
* [*Goethe-Schiller Monument*](/wiki/Goethe-Schiller_Monument_(Milwaukee) "Goethe-Schiller Monument (Milwaukee)")
* [*Immigrant Mother*](/wiki/Immigrant_Mother_(sculpture) "Immigrant Mother (sculpture)")
* *[Letter Carriers' Monument](/wiki/Letter_Carriers%27_Monument "Letter Carriers' Monument")*, a memorial to the [National Association of Letter Carriers](/wiki/National_Association_of_Letter_Carriers "National Association of Letter Carriers")

Additionally, Milwaukee has a burgeoning mural arts scene. [Black Cat Alley](/wiki/Black_Cat_Alley "Black Cat Alley") is a well-known arts destination in a one-block alleyway in the [East Side](/wiki/The_East_Side_(Milwaukee) "The East Side (Milwaukee)") neighborhood of Milwaukee, recognized for its street art mural installations. It is behind the historic [Oriental Theatre](/wiki/Oriental_Theatre_(Milwaukee) "Oriental Theatre (Milwaukee)") and includes both temporary and semi-permanent installations by a variety of artists and art groups. Another highly visible corridor of street art in Milwaukee is on the south side in the Walker's Point neighborhood, especially along 5th and 2nd streets.

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Generac.jpg/250px-Generac.jpg)](/wiki/File:Generac.jpg)

[Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest") is an annual music festival held at [Henry Maier Festival Park](/wiki/Henry_Maier_Festival_Park "Henry Maier Festival Park").

The city hosts an annual lakefront music festival called [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest").[[146]](#cite_note-152) Listed in the *1999 [Guinness Book of World Records](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_World_Records "Guinness Book of World Records")* as the largest [music festival](/wiki/Music_festival "Music festival") in the world, in 2017 Summerfest attracted 831,769.[[147]](#cite_note-153) The adjacent city of [West Allis](/wiki/West_Allis "West Allis") has been the site of the [Wisconsin State Fair](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Fair "Wisconsin State Fair") for over a century.

Milwaukee hosts a variety of primarily ethnically themed festivals throughout the summer. Held generally on the lakefront [Summerfest grounds](/wiki/Henry_Maier_Festival_Park "Henry Maier Festival Park"), these festivals span several days (typically Friday plus the weekend) and celebrate Milwaukee's history and diversity. Festivals for the [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") ([PrideFest](/wiki/PrideFest_(Milwaukee) "PrideFest (Milwaukee)")) and [Polish](/wiki/Polish-Americans "Polish-Americans") ([Polish Fest](/wiki/Polish_Fest "Polish Fest")) communities are typically held in June. Summerfest spans 11 days at the end of June and beginning of July. There are [French](/wiki/French-Americans "French-Americans") (Bastille Days), [Greek](/wiki/Greek-Americans "Greek-Americans"), [Italian](/wiki/Italian-Americans "Italian-Americans") ([Festa Italiana](/wiki/Festa_Italiana "Festa Italiana")) and [German](/wiki/German-Americans "German-Americans") ([German Fest](/wiki/German_Fest "German Fest")) festivals in July. The [African](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), [Arab](/wiki/Arab-Americans "Arab-Americans"), [Irish](/wiki/Irish-Americans "Irish-Americans") ([Irish Fest](/wiki/Irish_Fest "Irish Fest")), [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican-Americans "Mexican-Americans"), and [American Indian](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") events wrap it up from August through September.[[148]](#cite_note-154) Milwaukee is host to the annual [Film Girl Film Festival](/wiki/Film_Girl_Film_Festival "Film Girl Film Festival") in the fall, celebrating shorts and features directed by women. Milwaukee is also home to [Trainfest](/wiki/Trainfest_(Milwaukee) "Trainfest (Milwaukee)"), the largest operating model railroad show in America, in November.

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

See also: [Candy Raisins](/wiki/Candy_Raisins "Candy Raisins")

Milwaukee's ethnic cuisines include [German](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Germany "Cuisine of Germany"), [Italian](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Italy "Cuisine of Italy"), [Russian](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Russia "Cuisine of Russia"), [Hmong](/wiki/Hmong_cuisine "Hmong cuisine"), [French](/wiki/Cuisine_of_France "Cuisine of France"), [Serbian](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Serbia "Cuisine of Serbia"), [Polish](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Poland "Cuisine of Poland"), [Thai](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Thailand "Cuisine of Thailand"), [Chinese](/wiki/Cuisine_of_China "Cuisine of China"), [Mexican](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Mexico "Cuisine of Mexico"), [Indian](/wiki/Cuisine_of_India "Cuisine of India"), [Korean](/wiki/Korean_Cuisine "Korean Cuisine"), [Japanese](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Japan "Cuisine of Japan"), Vietnamese, Turkish, Middle Eastern, and Ethiopian.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Milwaukee County hosts the Zoo-A La Carte at the [Milwaukee County Zoo](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Zoo "Milwaukee County Zoo"),[[149]](#cite_note-155)[[150]](#cite_note-156) and various ethnic festivals like [Irish Fest](/wiki/Milwaukee_Irish_Fest "Milwaukee Irish Fest"), [Polish Fest](/wiki/Polish_Fest "Polish Fest"), [German Fest](/wiki/German_Fest "German Fest"), and [Festa Italiana](/wiki/Festa_Italiana "Festa Italiana") celebrate various types of cuisine in summer months.[[151]](#cite_note-157)

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Jazz_in_the_park_Milwaukee_6062.jpg/250px-Jazz_in_the_park_Milwaukee_6062.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jazz_in_the_park_Milwaukee_6062.jpg)

Aerial view of "Jazz in the Park", [Cathedral Square Park](/wiki/Parks_of_Milwaukee "Parks of Milwaukee")

Milwaukee has a long history of musical activity. The first organized musical society, called "Milwaukee Beethoven Society" formed in 1843, three years before the city was incorporated.[[152]](#cite_note-158)

The large concentrations of German and other European immigrants contributed to the musical character of the city. [Saengerfeste](/wiki/Saengerfest "Saengerfest") were held regularly.[[153]](#cite_note-159)

In the early 20th century, guitarist [Les Paul](/wiki/Les_Paul "Les Paul") and pianist [Liberace](/wiki/Liberace "Liberace") were some of the area's most famous musicians. Both Paul, born in Waukesha, and Liberace, born in West Allis, launched their careers in Milwaukee music venues. [Paramount Records](/wiki/Paramount_Records "Paramount Records"), primarily a jazz and blues record label, was founded in [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton,_Wisconsin "Grafton, Wisconsin"), a northern suburb of Milwaukee, and operated active between 1917 and 1932. [Hal Leonard Corporation](/wiki/Hal_Leonard_Corporation "Hal Leonard Corporation"), founded in 1947, is one of the world's largest music print publishers, and is headquartered in Milwaukee.[[154]](#cite_note-160) More recently, Milwaukee has a history of [rock](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop"), [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"), [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"), [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), [industrial music](/wiki/Industrial_music "Industrial music"), [electronica](/wiki/Electronica "Electronica"), [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"), and [pop music](/wiki/Pop_music "Pop music") bands.

Milwaukee's most famous music venue is [Summerfest](/wiki/Summerfest "Summerfest"). Founded in 1968, Summerfest features 700–800 live musical acts across 12 stages during 11 days over a 12-day period beginning in late June; while the dates adjust each year, Summerfest always includes July 4. On the Summerfest grounds, the largest venue is the American Family Insurance Amphitheater with a 23,000 person capacity. Adjacent is the BMO Harris Pavilion, which has a capacity of roughly 10,000. The BMO Harris Pavilion also hosts numerous concerts and events outside of Summerfest; other stages are also used during the numerous other festivals held on the grounds.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Pabst_Theater_1895_front_view_2012.jpg/250px-Pabst_Theater_1895_front_view_2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pabst_Theater_1895_front_view_2012.jpg)

[Pabst Theater](/wiki/Pabst_Theater "Pabst Theater")

Venues such as [Pabst Theater](/wiki/Pabst_Theater "Pabst Theater"), [Marcus Center](/wiki/Marcus_Center "Marcus Center") for Performing Arts, the [Helene Zelazo Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Helene_Zelazo_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Helene Zelazo Center for the Performing Arts"), [Marcus Amphitheater](/wiki/Marcus_Amphitheater "Marcus Amphitheater") ([Summerfest Grounds](/wiki/Summerfest_Grounds "Summerfest Grounds")), [Riverside Theater](/wiki/Riverside_Theater_(Milwaukee) "Riverside Theater (Milwaukee)"), the [Northern Lights Theater](/wiki/Potawatomi_Bingo_Casino "Potawatomi Bingo Casino"), and [The Rave](/wiki/The_Rave "The Rave") frequently bring internationally known acts to Milwaukee. 'Jazz in the Park', a weekly jazz show held at downtown [Cathedral Square Park](/wiki/Parks_of_Milwaukee "Parks of Milwaukee"), has become a summer tradition; free, public performances with a picnic environment.[[155]](#cite_note-161) Nearby Pere Marquette Park hosts "River Rhythms" on Wednesday nights.

Musicians and musical acts originating in the Milwaukee area include [Steve Miller](/wiki/Steve_Miller_(musician) "Steve Miller (musician)") (rock), Wladziu Valentino [Liberace](/wiki/Liberace "Liberace") (piano), [Al Jarreau](/wiki/Al_Jarreau "Al Jarreau") (jazz), [Eric Benet](/wiki/Eric_Benet "Eric Benet") (neo-soul), [Speech](/wiki/Speech_(rapper) "Speech (rapper)") (hip-hop), [Daryl Stuermer](/wiki/Daryl_Stuermer "Daryl Stuermer") (rock), [Streetz-n-Young Deuces](/wiki/Streetz-n-Young_Deuces "Streetz-n-Young Deuces") (hip-hop), [BoDeans](/wiki/BoDeans "BoDeans") (rock), [Les Paul](/wiki/Les_Paul "Les Paul") (jazz), the [Violent Femmes](/wiki/Violent_Femmes "Violent Femmes") (alternative), [Coo Coo Cal](/wiki/Coo_Coo_Cal "Coo Coo Cal") (rap), [Die Kreuzen](/wiki/Die_Kreuzen "Die Kreuzen") (punk), [Andy Hurley](/wiki/Andy_Hurley "Andy Hurley") of [Fall Out Boy](/wiki/Fall_Out_Boy "Fall Out Boy") (punk), [Rico Love](/wiki/Rico_Love "Rico Love") (R&B), [Andrew 'The Butcher' Mrotek](/wiki/Andrew_Mrotek "Andrew Mrotek") of [The Academy Is...](/wiki/The_Academy_Is... "The Academy Is...") (alt-rock), Showoff (pop-punk), [The Promise Ring](/wiki/The_Promise_Ring "The Promise Ring") (indie), [Lights Out Asia](/wiki/Lights_Out_Asia "Lights Out Asia") (post-rock), [the Gufs](/wiki/The_Gufs "The Gufs") (alt rock), Brief Candles (rock), [IshDARR](/wiki/IshDARR "IshDARR") (rap), [Decibully](/wiki/Decibully "Decibully") (indie), and [Reyna](/wiki/Reyna_(musical_group) "Reyna (musical group)") (synth-pop).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[importance?](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")*]

The city is represented in two of the four [major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada")–the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers") of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") and the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") of the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"). Milwaukee does not have a [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") team or a [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") team. For a short time, Milwaukee was home to the NFL's [Badgers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Badgers "Milwaukee Badgers"), from 1922 to 1926. Today, the city is generally considered a second home market for the NFL's [Green Bay Packers](/wiki/Green_Bay_Packers "Green Bay Packers").[[157]](#cite_note-163) The team split its home schedule between [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") and Milwaukee from 1933 to 1994, although most home games during that time were played in Green Bay. Of the games played in Milwaukee, the majority were played at [County Stadium](/wiki/County_Stadium "County Stadium").[[158]](#cite_note-164) However, by 1991, the Packers claimed that revenue from the Milwaukee games were 60% of the revenue generated from the Green Bay games, and Milwaukee officials did not act upon the Packers' request that County Stadium be replaced with an updated stadium.[[159]](#cite_note-165) The Packers' longtime [flagship station](/wiki/Flagship_station "Flagship station") is Milwaukee-based [WTMJ AM 620](/wiki/WTMJ_(AM) "WTMJ (AM)").[[160]](#cite_note-166)

Milwaukee also has a strong history of nonprofessional sports dating back to the 19th century. [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") watched [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") in Milwaukee in 1849 when he attended a game between Chicago and Milwaukee. In 1854, the Milwaukee Cricket Club had 150 members.[[161]](#cite_note-167)

Milwaukee was the host city of the [International Cycling Classic](/wiki/International_Cycling_Classic "International Cycling Classic"), which included the men's and women's *Superweek* Pro Tour races, featuring cyclists and teams from across the United States and more than 20 other countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

Main article: [List of parks in Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_parks_in_Milwaukee "List of parks in Milwaukee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Milwaukee_August_2024_4_%28Veterans_Park_Lagoon%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_August_2024_4_%28Veterans_Park_Lagoon%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_August_2024_4_(Veterans_Park_Lagoon).jpg)

Veterans Park lagoon

Milwaukee is known for its well-developed Milwaukee County Parks system.[[162]](#cite_note-168) The "Grand Necklace of Parks", designed by famed landscape architect [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted"), includes [Lake Park](/wiki/Lake_Park,_Milwaukee "Lake Park, Milwaukee") and [Bradford Beach](/wiki/Bradford_Beach "Bradford Beach"), River Park (now Riverside Park), and West Park (now [Washington Park](/wiki/Washington_Park,_Milwaukee "Washington Park, Milwaukee")). Milwaukee County Parks offer facilities for sunbathing, picnics, grilling, [disc golf](/wiki/Disc_golf "Disc golf"), and ice skating.[[163]](#cite_note-mcpsite-169) Milwaukee has over 140 parks with over 15,000 acres (6,100 ha) of parks and parkways. During the summer months, Cathedral Park in downtown Milwaukee hosts "Jazz in the Park" on Thursday nights.[[164]](#cite_note-170) Nearby Pere Marquette Park hosts "[River Rhythms](/wiki/River_Rhythms_(Wisconsin) "River Rhythms (Wisconsin)")" on Wednesday nights.

The [Milwaukee Riverwalk](/wiki/Milwaukee_Riverwalk "Milwaukee Riverwalk") is a continuous pedestrian walkway along the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River") containing art displays, cafés and restaurants.[[165]](#cite_note-171) It extends from the [Historic Third Ward](/wiki/Historic_Third_Ward,_Milwaukee "Historic Third Ward, Milwaukee") district to [Caesars Park](/wiki/Parks_of_Milwaukee "Parks of Milwaukee") near Brady Street. It also links to the [Hank Aaron State Trail](/wiki/Hank_Aaron_State_Trail "Hank Aaron State Trail"), Lakeshore State Park, and [Erie Street Plaza](/wiki/Erie_Street_Plaza "Erie Street Plaza").[[166]](#cite_note-172) The Hank Aaron State Trail is a 15.2-mile (24.5 km) [shared-use path](/wiki/Shared-use_path "Shared-use path") that travels east-west between Lakeshore State Park to [Wauwatosa](/wiki/Wauwatosa,_Wisconsin "Wauwatosa, Wisconsin") via the [Menomonee Valley](/wiki/Menomonee_Valley "Menomonee Valley").[[167]](#cite_note-173)

[Lakeshore State Park](/wiki/Lakeshore_State_Park "Lakeshore State Park") consists of 22 acres (8.9 ha) on the shores of Lake Michigan.[[168]](#cite_note-:0-174) It is situated adjacent to both [Discovery World](/wiki/Discovery_World_(museum) "Discovery World (museum)") and [Henry Maier Festival Park](/wiki/Henry_Maier_Festival_Park "Henry Maier Festival Park").[[169]](#cite_note-:1-175)[[170]](#cite_note-:2-176) It is the only urban state park in Wisconsin and features [restored prairie](/wiki/Prairie_restoration "Prairie restoration") and a pebble beach.[[168]](#cite_note-:0-174)

### Nature centers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Nature centers")]

Milwaukee's parks are home to several nature centers. [Mitchell Park Horticultural Conservatory](/wiki/Mitchell_Park_Horticultural_Conservatory "Mitchell Park Horticultural Conservatory") is composed of three beehive-shaped glass domes that span 140 feet (43 m) in diameter and are 85 feet (26 m) high. They are properly referred to as the world's first conoidal domes.[[171]](#cite_note-177) They cover 45,000 square feet (4,200 m2) of display area and were constructed in stages from 1959 to 1967. The conservatory includes the Tropical Dome, the Arid Dome and the Show Dome, which hosts four seasonal (cultural, literary, or historic) shows and one Christmas-themed exhibit held annually in December.

The [Urban Ecology Center](/wiki/Urban_Ecology_Center "Urban Ecology Center") offers programming for adults and children from its three branches located in Riverside Park, Washington Park, and the [Menomonee Valley](/wiki/Menomonee_Valley "Menomonee Valley") (near Three Bridges Park).[[172]](#cite_note-178) The Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources operates a nature center at [Havenwoods State Forest](/wiki/Havenwoods_State_Forest "Havenwoods State Forest").[[173]](#cite_note-179) The city is also served by two nearby suburban nature centers. [Wehr Nature Center](/wiki/Wehr_Nature_Center "Wehr Nature Center") is operated by Milwaukee County in [Whitnall Park](/wiki/Whitnall_Park "Whitnall Park"), spread across [Franklin](/wiki/Franklin,_Milwaukee_County,_Wisconsin "Franklin, Milwaukee County, Wisconsin"), [Greendale](/wiki/Greendale,_Wisconsin "Greendale, Wisconsin") and [Hales Corners](/wiki/Hales_Corners "Hales Corners"). Admission is free, and parking costs $5 per vehicle.[[174]](#cite_note-180) The Schlitz Audubon Nature Center in [Bayside, Wisconsin](/wiki/Bayside,_Wisconsin "Bayside, Wisconsin"), charges admittance fees for visitors.[[175]](#cite_note-181)

The Monarch Trail, on the Milwaukee County Grounds in Wauwatosa, is a 1.25-mile (2 km) trail that highlights the fall migration of the [monarch butterflies](/wiki/Monarch_butterflies "Monarch butterflies").[[176]](#cite_note-182)

### Markets

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Markets")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Milwaukee_September_2023_022_%28Milwaukee_Public_Market%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_September_2023_022_%28Milwaukee_Public_Market%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_September_2023_022_(Milwaukee_Public_Market).jpg)

[Milwaukee Public Market](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Market "Milwaukee Public Market")

[Milwaukee Public Market](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Market "Milwaukee Public Market"), in the [Third Ward](/wiki/Historic_Third_Ward_(Milwaukee) "Historic Third Ward (Milwaukee)") neighborhood, is an indoor market that sells produce, seafood, meats, cheeses, vegetables, candies, and flowers from local businesses. It opened in 2005 and drew inspiration from [Pike Place Market](/wiki/Pike_Place_Market "Pike Place Market") in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), envisioning a space to support local vendors.[[177]](#cite_note-183)

Milwaukee County Farmers Markets, held in season, sell fresh produce, meats, cheeses, jams, jellies, preserves and syrups, and plants. Farmers markets also feature artists and craftspeople. Locations include: Aur Farmers Market, Brown Deer Farmers Market, Cudahy Farmers Market, East Town Farm Market, Enderis Park Farmers Market, Fondy Farmers Market, Mitchell Street Market, Riverwest Gardeners' Market, Silver Spring Farmers Market, South Milwaukee Farmers Market, South Shore Farmers Market, Uptown Farmers Market, Wauwatosa Farmers Market, West Allis Farmers Market, and Westown Market on the Park.

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Government of Milwaukee](/wiki/Government_of_Milwaukee "Government of Milwaukee")

See also: [List of mayors of Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Milwaukee "List of mayors of Milwaukee")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Milwaukee_City_Hall_742.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_City_Hall_742.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_City_Hall_742.jpg)

[Milwaukee City Hall](/wiki/Milwaukee_City_Hall "Milwaukee City Hall") was built in 1895 and based on German counterparts.

Milwaukee has a [mayor-council](/wiki/Mayor-council "Mayor-council") form of government. With the election of Mayor [John O. Norquist](/wiki/John_O._Norquist "John O. Norquist") in 1988, the city adopted a cabinet form of government with the mayor appointing department heads not otherwise elected or appointed—notably the Fire and Police Chiefs. While this gave the mayor greater control of the city's day-to-day operations, the Common Council retains almost complete control over the city's finances and the mayor, with the exception of his proposed annual budget, cannot directly introduce legislation.[[178]](#cite_note-184) The Common Council consists of 15 members, one from each district in the city, who serve a 4-year term.[[179]](#cite_note-185)

Milwaukee has a history of giving long tenures to its mayors; from [Frank Zeidler](/wiki/Frank_Zeidler "Frank Zeidler") to [Tom Barrett](/wiki/Tom_Barrett_(Wisconsin_politician) "Tom Barrett (Wisconsin politician)"), the city had only four elected mayors (and one acting) in a 73-year period.[[180]](#cite_note-186) When 28-year incumbent [Henry Maier](/wiki/Henry_Maier "Henry Maier") retired in 1988, he held the record for longest term of service for a city of Milwaukee's size,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and when Barrett retired in 2021, he was the longest-serving mayor of any of the United States' 50 largest cities.[[181]](#cite_note-187)

In addition to the election of a Mayor and Common Council on the city level, Milwaukee residents elect county representatives to the [Milwaukee County](/wiki/Milwaukee_County "Milwaukee County") [Board of Supervisors](/wiki/Board_of_Supervisors "Board of Supervisors"), as well as a Milwaukee County Executive. The current [County Executive](/wiki/County_Executive "County Executive") is [David Crowley](/wiki/David_Crowley_(Wisconsin_politician) "David Crowley (Wisconsin politician)"), who was reelected in 2024 after being first elected to the position in 2020.[[182]](#cite_note-188)

Milwaukee has been a [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") stronghold for more than a century at the federal level.[[183]](#cite_note-189) At the local level, [Socialists](/wiki/Socialist_Party_of_America "Socialist Party of America") often won the mayorship and (for briefer periods) other city and county offices during much of the first sixty years of the 20th century. The city is split between seven [State Senate](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Senate "Wisconsin State Senate") districts, each of which is divided between three [state Assembly](/wiki/Wisconsin_State_Assembly "Wisconsin State Assembly") districts. All but four state legislators representing the city are Democrats; the four [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")—two in the State Assembly and two in the State Senate—represent outer portions of the city that are part of districts dominated by heavily Republican suburban counties. In 2008, [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") won Milwaukee with 77% of the vote.[[184]](#cite_note-190) [Tim Carpenter](/wiki/Tim_Carpenter "Tim Carpenter") (D), [Lena Taylor](/wiki/Lena_Taylor "Lena Taylor") (D), [Robyn Vining](/wiki/Robyn_Vining "Robyn Vining") (D), [LaTonya Johnson](/wiki/LaTonya_Johnson "LaTonya Johnson") (D), [Chris Larson](/wiki/Chris_Larson "Chris Larson") (D), [Alberta Darling](/wiki/Alberta_Darling "Alberta Darling") (R), and [Dave Craig](/wiki/Dave_Craig "Dave Craig") (R) represent Milwaukee in the State Senate; [Daniel Riemer](/wiki/Daniel_Riemer "Daniel Riemer") (D), [JoCasta Zamarripa](/wiki/JoCasta_Zamarripa "JoCasta Zamarripa") (D), [Marisabel Cabrera](/wiki/Marisabel_Cabrera "Marisabel Cabrera") (D), [David Bowen](/wiki/David_Bowen_(Wisconsin) "David Bowen (Wisconsin)") (D), [Jason Fields](/wiki/Jason_Fields "Jason Fields") (D), [LaKeshia Myers](/wiki/LaKeshia_Myers "LaKeshia Myers") (D), [Sara Rodriguez](/wiki/Sara_Rodriguez "Sara Rodriguez") (D), [Dale P. Kooyenga](/wiki/Dale_P._Kooyenga "Dale P. Kooyenga") (R), [Kalan Haywood](/wiki/Kalan_Haywood "Kalan Haywood") (D), [David Crowley](/wiki/David_Crowley_(Wisconsin_politician) "David Crowley (Wisconsin politician)") (D), [Evan Goyke](/wiki/Evan_Goyke "Evan Goyke") (D), [Jonathan Brostoff](/wiki/Jonathan_Brostoff "Jonathan Brostoff") (D), [Christine Sinicki](/wiki/Christine_Sinicki "Christine Sinicki") (D), [Janel Brandtjen](/wiki/Janel_Brandtjen "Janel Brandtjen") (R), and [Mike Kuglitsch](/wiki/Mike_Kuglitsch "Mike Kuglitsch") (R) represent Milwaukee in the State Assembly.

Milwaukee makes up the overwhelming majority of [Wisconsin's 4th congressional district](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_4th_congressional_district "Wisconsin's 4th congressional district"). The district is heavily Democratic, with victory in the Democratic primary often being considered [tantamount to election](/wiki/Tantamount_to_election "Tantamount to election").[[185]](#cite_note-191) The district is currently represented by Democrat [Gwen Moore](/wiki/Gwen_Moore "Gwen Moore"). A Republican has not represented a significant portion of Milwaukee in Congress since [Charles J. Kersten](/wiki/Charles_J._Kersten "Charles J. Kersten") lost his seat in the [5th district](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_5th_congressional_district "Wisconsin's 5th congressional district") in 1954 to Democrat [Henry S. Reuss](/wiki/Henry_S._Reuss "Henry S. Reuss"). The small portions of the city extending into Waukesha and Washington counties are part of the [5th District](/wiki/Wisconsin%27s_5th_congressional_district "Wisconsin's 5th congressional district"), represented by Republican [Scott L. Fitzgerald](/wiki/Scott_L._Fitzgerald "Scott L. Fitzgerald").

Milwaukee's Mexican Consulate serves 65 counties in Wisconsin and the [Upper Peninsula of Michigan](/wiki/Upper_Peninsula_of_Michigan "Upper Peninsula of Michigan").[[186]](#cite_note-192)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Crime")]

In 2001 and 2007, Milwaukee ranked among the ten most dangerous large cities in the United States.[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-194) Despite its improvement since then, as of 2012 Milwaukee fared worse when comparing specific crime types to the national average (e.g., homicide, rape, robbery, aggravated assault)[[189]](#cite_note-195)[[190]](#cite_note-196)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]
The [Milwaukee Police Department](/wiki/Milwaukee_Police_Department "Milwaukee Police Department")'s Gang Unit was reactivated in 2004 after [Nannette Hegerty](/wiki/Nannette_Hegerty "Nannette Hegerty") was sworn in as chief. In 2006, 4,000 charges were brought against suspects through Milwaukee's Gang Unit.[[191]](#cite_note-197) In 2013 there were 105 murders in Milwaukee and 87 homicides the following year.[[192]](#cite_note-198) In 2015, 146 people were killed in the city.[[193]](#cite_note-199) In 2020, Milwaukee was ranked the tenth most dangerous city in the US.[[194]](#cite_note-200) In 2022, Milwaukee set a homicide record with 214 homicides.[[195]](#cite_note-201)

### Poverty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Poverty")]

As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit), Milwaukee currently ranks as the second poorest U.S. city with over 500,000 residents, falling behind only [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit").[[196]](#cite_note-202) In 2013, a Point-In-Time survey estimated 1,500 people were homeless on Milwaukee's streets each night,[[197]](#cite_note-203) although as of 2022 the estimate has reduced to 832.[[198]](#cite_note-204) The city's homeless and poor are aided by several local nonprofits, including the [Milwaukee Rescue Mission](/wiki/Milwaukee_Rescue_Mission "Milwaukee Rescue Mission").
