## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/UWM-0904-downer-quad.jpg/250px-UWM-0904-downer-quad.jpg)](/wiki/File:UWM-0904-downer-quad.jpg)

The [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee") in [Downer Woods](/wiki/East_Side_(Milwaukee)#Downer_Woods "East Side (Milwaukee)") is the largest university in the city.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Marquette_University.jpg/250px-Marquette_University.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marquette_University.jpg)

Near downtown, [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University") is Wisconsin's largest private university by enrollment.

Of persons in Milwaukee aged 25 and above, 89.2% have a high school diploma and 32.4% have a bachelor's degree or higher.[[5]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-5)

### Primary and secondary education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Primary and secondary education")]

[Milwaukee Public Schools](/wiki/Milwaukee_Public_Schools "Milwaukee Public Schools") (MPS) includes almost all of the city of Milwaukee.[[205]](#cite_note-211) It is the largest school district in Wisconsin and [among the largest](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_school_districts_in_the_United_States_by_enrollment "List of the largest school districts in the United States by enrollment") in the nation; it had an enrollment of 69,115 as of the 2021-2022 academic year. Milwaukee Public Schools operate as [magnet schools](/wiki/Magnet_school "Magnet school"), with individualized specialty areas for interests in academics or the arts. The district operates 156 schools, including 18 high schools.[[206]](#cite_note-212)

Milwaukee is also home to over two dozen private or [parochial](/wiki/Parochial_school "Parochial school") high schools and numerous private middle and elementary schools. Eight high schools are operated by the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_schools_in_the_Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Milwaukee "List of schools in the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Milwaukee"). In 1990, Milwaukee became the first city in the United States to offer a [school voucher](/wiki/School_voucher "School voucher") program.[[207]](#cite_note-213)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Higher education")]

The [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee") is a public research university in the [Downer Woods](/wiki/East_Side_(Milwaukee)#Downer_Woods "East Side (Milwaukee)") neighborhood and the largest college or university in the city.[[208]](#cite_note-214) [Marquette University](/wiki/Marquette_University "Marquette University"), a private [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") institution near downtown Milwaukee founded in 1881, is the state's largest private college.[[209]](#cite_note-215) Discipline-specific institutions include the [Medical College of Wisconsin](/wiki/Medical_College_of_Wisconsin "Medical College of Wisconsin"), [Milwaukee Institute of Art and Design](/wiki/Milwaukee_Institute_of_Art_and_Design "Milwaukee Institute of Art and Design") and [Milwaukee School of Engineering](/wiki/Milwaukee_School_of_Engineering "Milwaukee School of Engineering"). The public [Milwaukee Area Technical College](/wiki/Milwaukee_Area_Technical_College "Milwaukee Area Technical College") provides technical and vocational education. Women's colleges include [Alverno College](/wiki/Alverno_College "Alverno College") and [Mount Mary University](/wiki/Mount_Mary_University "Mount Mary University"). Other higher education institutions in the city and its suburbs include [Bryant & Stratton College](/wiki/Bryant_%26_Stratton_College "Bryant & Stratton College"), [Carroll University](/wiki/Carroll_University "Carroll University"), [Concordia University Wisconsin](/wiki/Concordia_University_Wisconsin "Concordia University Wisconsin"), [Herzing University](/wiki/Herzing_University "Herzing University"), [Nashotah House](/wiki/Nashotah_House "Nashotah House"), [Saint Francis de Sales Seminary](/wiki/Saint_Francis_de_Sales_Seminary "Saint Francis de Sales Seminary") and [Wisconsin Lutheran College](/wiki/Wisconsin_Lutheran_College "Wisconsin Lutheran College").

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Media")]

See also: [List of television stations in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_television_stations_in_Wisconsin "List of television stations in Wisconsin") and [List of radio stations in Wisconsin](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_Wisconsin "List of radio stations in Wisconsin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Witi_tv_tower_shorewood_wisconsin.JPG/250px-Witi_tv_tower_shorewood_wisconsin.JPG)](/wiki/File:Witi_tv_tower_shorewood_wisconsin.JPG)

The [WITI TV Tower](/wiki/WITI_TV_Tower "WITI TV Tower") is in Shorewood, off of the [Oak Leaf Trail](/wiki/Oak_Leaf_Trail "Oak Leaf Trail"), just north of [Capitol Drive](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_190 "Wisconsin Highway 190").

Milwaukee's daily [newspaper](/wiki/Newspaper "Newspaper") is the *[Milwaukee Journal Sentinel](/wiki/Milwaukee_Journal_Sentinel "Milwaukee Journal Sentinel")*, which was formed when the morning paper the *Milwaukee Sentinel* merged with the afternoon paper *Milwaukee Journal*. The city has two free distribution alternative publications, *[Shepherd Express](/wiki/Shepherd_Express "Shepherd Express")* and *[Wisconsin Gazette](/w/index.php?title=Wisconsin_Gazette&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wisconsin Gazette (page does not exist)")*. Other local newspapers, city guides, and magazines with large distributions include *[Milwaukee Magazine](/wiki/Milwaukee_Magazine "Milwaukee Magazine")*, *Milwaukee Neighborhood News Service*, *Milwaukee Independent*, *Riverwest Currents*, *The Milwaukee Courier* and *Milwaukee Community Journal*. [Urban Milwaukee](/wiki/Urban_Milwaukee "Urban Milwaukee") and [OnMilwaukee.com](/wiki/OnMilwaukee.com "OnMilwaukee.com") are online-only publications providing political and real-estate news as well as stories about cultural events and entertainment. The *[UWM Post](/wiki/UWM_Post "UWM Post")* is the independent, student-run weekly at the [University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee](/wiki/University_of_Wisconsin%E2%80%93Milwaukee "University of Wisconsin–Milwaukee").[[210]](#cite_note-216)

Milwaukee's major [network television](/wiki/Network_television "Network television") affiliates are [WTMJ](/wiki/WTMJ-TV "WTMJ-TV") 4 ([NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC")), [WITI](/wiki/WITI-TV "WITI-TV") 6 ([Fox](/wiki/Fox_Broadcasting_Company "Fox Broadcasting Company")), [WISN](/wiki/WISN-TV "WISN-TV") 12 ([ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company")), [WVTV](/wiki/WVTV "WVTV") 18/24 ([CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW")/[MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV")), and [WDJT](/wiki/WDJT "WDJT") 58 ([CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS")). Spanish-language programming is on [WTSJ-LD](/wiki/WTSJ-LD "WTSJ-LD") 38 ([Visión Latina](/wiki/Visi%C3%B3n_Latina "Visión Latina")) and [WYTU-LD](/wiki/WYTU-LD "WYTU-LD") 63 ([Telemundo](/wiki/Telemundo "Telemundo")). Milwaukee's [public broadcasting stations](/wiki/Milwaukee_PBS "Milwaukee PBS") are [WMVS](/wiki/WMVS "WMVS") 10 and [WMVT](/wiki/WMVT "WMVT") 36.

Other television stations in the Milwaukee market include [WMKE-CD](/wiki/WMKE-CD "WMKE-CD") 7 ([Quest](/wiki/Quest_(U.S._TV_network) "Quest (U.S. TV network)")), [WVCY](/wiki/WVCY-TV "WVCY-TV") 30 ([FN](/wiki/FamilyNet "FamilyNet")), [WBME-CD](/wiki/WBME-CD "WBME-CD") 41 ([Me-TV](/wiki/Me-TV "Me-TV")), [WMLW-TV](/wiki/WMLW-TV "WMLW-TV") 49 ([Independent](/wiki/Independent_station "Independent station")), [WWRS](/wiki/WWRS "WWRS") 52 ([TBN](/wiki/Trinity_Broadcasting_Network "Trinity Broadcasting Network")), [Sportsman Channel](/wiki/Sportsman_Channel "Sportsman Channel"), and [WPXE](/wiki/WPXE "WPXE") 55 ([ION](/wiki/ION_Television "ION Television"))

There are numerous [radio](/wiki/Radio "Radio") stations throughout Milwaukee and the surrounding area.

There are two cable [PEG](/wiki/Public,_educational,_and_government_access "Public, educational, and government access") channels in Milwaukee: channels 13 and 25.

Until 2015, [Journal Communications](/wiki/Journal_Communications "Journal Communications") (a [NYSE](/wiki/NYSE "NYSE")-traded corporation) published the *Journal Sentinel* and well over a dozen local weekly newspapers in the metropolitan area. At that time, Journal was split into the Journal Media Group for publishing, while the television and radio stations went to the [E. W. Scripps Company](/wiki/E._W._Scripps_Company "E. W. Scripps Company") (Journal founded WTMJ-TV, along with [WTMJ](/wiki/WTMJ_(AM) "WTMJ (AM)") and [WKTI](/wiki/WKTI "WKTI")). As a result, it was criticized for having a near-[monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly") in local news coverage.[[211]](#cite_note-217)[[212]](#cite_note-218) Journal Media Group merged with [Gannett](/wiki/Gannett "Gannett") in 2016, while Scripps sold the radio stations in 2018 to [Good Karma Brands](/wiki/Good_Karma_Brands "Good Karma Brands"), effectively splitting off the monopoly completely.

The city is the home of [Red Letter Media](/wiki/Red_Letter_Media "Red Letter Media"), independent filmmakers responsible for such works as *[Space Cop](/wiki/Space_Cop "Space Cop")*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

### Health care

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Health care")]

Milwaukee's health care industry includes several health systems. The Milwaukee Regional Medical Complex, between 8700 and 9200 West Wisconsin Avenue, is on the Milwaukee County grounds. This area includes the [Children's Hospital of Wisconsin](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_of_Wisconsin "Children's Hospital of Wisconsin"), [Froedtert Hospital](/wiki/Froedtert_Hospital "Froedtert Hospital"), BloodCenter of Wisconsin, the [Ronald McDonald House](/wiki/Ronald_McDonald_House "Ronald McDonald House"), Curative Rehabilitation, and the [Medical College of Wisconsin](/wiki/Medical_College_of_Wisconsin "Medical College of Wisconsin"). [Aurora Health Care](/wiki/Aurora_Health_Care "Aurora Health Care") includes [St. Luke's Medical Center](/wiki/Aurora_St._Luke%27s_Medical_Center "Aurora St. Luke's Medical Center"), Aurora Sinai Medical Center, Aurora West Allis Medical Center, and St. Luke's SouthShore. [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_(healthcare_system) "Ascension (healthcare system)") includes St. Joseph's Hospital, St. Francis Hospital, St. Mary's Hospital, The Wisconsin Heart Hospital, Elmbrook Memorial (Brookfield), and other outpatient clinics in the Milwaukee area. The Medical College of Wisconsin is one of two medical schools in Wisconsin and the only one in Milwaukee.

Other health care non-profit organizations in Milwaukee include national headquarters of the [American Academy of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Allergy,_Asthma,_and_Immunology "American Academy of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology") and the Endometriosis Association.

### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Mke-airport-terminal.jpg/250px-Mke-airport-terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mke-airport-terminal.jpg)

Main terminal at [Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mitchell_International_Airport "Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport")

According to the 2022 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), 66% of working city of Milwaukee residents commuted by driving alone, 11.1% carpooled, 4.5% used public transportation, and 4.3% walked. About 2% used all other forms of transportation, including taxicab, motorcycle, and bicycle. About 12.1% of working city of Milwaukee residents worked at home.[[213]](#cite_note-219) In 2015, 17.9% of city of Milwaukee households were without a car, which increased to 18.7% in 2016. The national average was 8.7 percent in 2016. Milwaukee averaged 1.3 cars per household in 2016, compared to a national average of 1.8 per household.[[214]](#cite_note-220)

A 2015 study by [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") ranked Milwaukee as the 15th most walkable out of the 50 largest U.S. cities.[[215]](#cite_note-221) As a whole, the city has a score of 62 out of 100. However, several of the more densely populated neighborhoods have much higher scores: Juneautown has a score of 95; the [Lower East Side](/wiki/The_East_Side_(Milwaukee) "The East Side (Milwaukee)") has a score of 91; Yankee Hill scored 91; and the Marquette and Murray Hill neighborhoods both scored 89 each.[[216]](#cite_note-222) Those ratings range from "A Walker's Paradise" to "Very Walkable."

#### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Airports")]

Milwaukee has two airports. [Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport](/wiki/Milwaukee_Mitchell_International_Airport "Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport") (KMKE) on the southern edge of the city handles the region's commercial traffic and is [categorized](/wiki/FAA_airport_categories "FAA airport categories") as a medium-hub primary commercial service facility.[[217]](#cite_note-NPIAS_Airports-223) [Lawrence J. Timmerman Airport](/wiki/Lawrence_J._Timmerman_Airport "Lawrence J. Timmerman Airport") (KMWC), known locally as Timmerman Field, is on the northwest side of the city and is categorized as a regional reliever airport facility, used mainly for general or private aviation.[[217]](#cite_note-NPIAS_Airports-223)

Mitchell International has one terminal with two concourses and 38 gates.[[218]](#cite_note-224) Its peak year of annual traffic was 9.8 million passengers in 2010; the airport served 6.3 million annual passengers in 2024.[[219]](#cite_note-225)[[220]](#cite_note-226) Mitchell is served by twelve airlines,[[221]](#cite_note-mitchellairport.com-227) which offer roughly 240 daily departures and 245 daily arrivals. Approximately 90 cities are served directly from Mitchell International. It is the largest airport in Wisconsin.[[222]](#cite_note-WisBusiness-228) The airport is connected to the Amtrak *[Hiawatha](/wiki/Hiawatha "Hiawatha")* train service.

#### Intercity rail and bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Intercity rail and bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station_7987.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station_7987.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station_7987.jpg)

[Milwaukee Intermodal Station](/wiki/Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station "Milwaukee Intermodal Station") is the city's intercity bus and train station.

Milwaukee's [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") station was renovated in 2007 to create [Milwaukee Intermodal Station](/wiki/Milwaukee_Intermodal_Station "Milwaukee Intermodal Station") near downtown Milwaukee and the Third Ward. The station replaced the previous main railway station, [Everett Street Depot](/wiki/Everett_Street_Depot "Everett Street Depot"), to improve access between Milwaukee's local transit and Amtrak riders. Milwaukee is served by Amtrak's *[Hiawatha](/wiki/Hiawatha_(Amtrak_train) "Hiawatha (Amtrak train)")* passenger train up to seven times daily between Milwaukee Intermodal Station and [Chicago Union Station](/wiki/Chicago_Union_Station "Chicago Union Station"). The *[Borealis](/wiki/Borealis_(train) "Borealis (train)")* provides daily service to Chicago and [Saint Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/Saint_Paul,_Minnesota "Saint Paul, Minnesota"), and is supplemental to the long-distance cross-country *[Empire Builder](/wiki/Empire_Builder "Empire Builder")*, connecting Milwaukee to [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon") and [Seattle, Washington](/wiki/Seattle,_Washington "Seattle, Washington").

In 2010, $800 million in federal funds were allocated to the creation of high-speed rail links from Milwaukee to Chicago and [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"), but the funds were rejected by Wisconsin governor [Scott Walker](/wiki/Scott_Walker_(politician) "Scott Walker (politician)").[[223]](#cite_note-229)[[224]](#cite_note-230) In 2016, [WisDOT](/wiki/WisDOT "WisDOT") and [IDOT](/wiki/IDOT "IDOT") conducted studies to upgrade service on the Amtrak *Hiawatha* line from seven to ten times daily between downtown Milwaukee and downtown Chicago.[[225]](#cite_note-231)[[226]](#cite_note-232) As a result of the 2021 infrastructure bill and the "Amtrak Connects Us" initiative, the Milwaukee Intermodal Station is again projected to serve passenger trains to Madison and Green Bay, with the goal of the new routes being operational by 2035.[[227]](#cite_note-233)

Intercity bus services to the city include [Amtrak Thruway](/wiki/Amtrak_Thruway "Amtrak Thruway"), [Badger Bus](/wiki/Badger_Bus "Badger Bus"), [Flixbus](/wiki/Flixbus "Flixbus"), [Greyhound Lines](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines"), [Indian Trails](/wiki/Indian_Trails "Indian Trails"), [Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines"), [Lamers Bus Lines](/w/index.php?title=Lamers_Bus_Lines&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lamers Bus Lines (page does not exist)"), [Megabus](/wiki/Megabus_(North_America) "Megabus (North America)"), [Wisconsin Coach Lines](/wiki/Wisconsin_Coach_Lines "Wisconsin Coach Lines") and other intercity bus operators.

#### Transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Transit")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Milwaukee_County_Transit_buses_5523_and_5104_eastbound_on_W._Wisconsin_Ave_%282018%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_County_Transit_buses_5523_and_5104_eastbound_on_W._Wisconsin_Ave_%282018%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_County_Transit_buses_5523_and_5104_eastbound_on_W._Wisconsin_Ave_(2018).jpg)

[Milwaukee County Transit System](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Transit_System "Milwaukee County Transit System") buses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Milwaukee_Hop_streetcar_02_at_Wisconsin_Ave_northbound_stop%2C_on_Milwaukee_St_%282022%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Hop_streetcar_02_at_Wisconsin_Ave_northbound_stop%2C_on_Milwaukee_St_%282022%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Hop_streetcar_02_at_Wisconsin_Ave_northbound_stop,_on_Milwaukee_St_(2022).jpg)

[The Hop](/wiki/The_Hop "The Hop") streetcar system

The [Milwaukee County Transit System](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Transit_System "Milwaukee County Transit System") provides bus services within Milwaukee County. The [Badger Bus](/wiki/Badger_Bus "Badger Bus") station in downtown Milwaukee provides bus service between Milwaukee and Madison. An East/West [Bus Rapid Transit (BRT)](/wiki/Bus_rapid_transit "Bus rapid transit") line between downtown and the Milwaukee Regional Medical Center is also currently under construction.[[228]](#cite_note-234)

A modern [streetcar](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar") system, [The Hop](/wiki/The_Hop "The Hop"), connects Milwaukee Intermodal Station, downtown Milwaukee, and Ogden Avenue on the city's [Lower East Side](/wiki/The_East_Side_(Milwaukee) "The East Side (Milwaukee)"). The initial M-Line opened for service on November 2, 2018.[[229]](#cite_note-235)[[230]](#cite_note-fox6now-236) Service to the lakefront, through the [Couture](/wiki/The_Couture "The Couture"), on the L-Line opened on October 29, 2023.[[231]](#cite_note-237)

Milwaukee has no commuter rail system. [Previous efforts to develop one](/wiki/Proposed_Kenosha%E2%80%93Racine%E2%80%93Milwaukee_regional_rail_service "Proposed Kenosha–Racine–Milwaukee regional rail service") proposed a 0.5% sales tax[[232]](#cite_note-238) in Milwaukee, [Racine](/wiki/Racine_County,_Wisconsin "Racine County, Wisconsin") and [Kenosha](/wiki/Kenosha_County,_Wisconsin "Kenosha County, Wisconsin") counties to fund an expansion of [Metra](/wiki/Metra "Metra")'s [Union Pacific North Line](/wiki/Union_Pacific_North_Line "Union Pacific North Line") to Milwaukee Intermodal Station.[[233]](#cite_note-239) A 1990s Wisconsin DOT plan determined the path forward for east-west transportation in Milwaukee to be a mix of a comprehensive light rail system, an expansion of I-94 with [HOV lanes](/wiki/HOV_lanes "HOV lanes"), and increased bus service to Waukesha County. Despite being awarded $289 million for this plan from the federal government, local Republican leaders rescinded support for light rail. The "locally preferred alternative" would have connected destinations including downtown Milwaukee, UW-Milwaukee, and the Milwaukee Regional Medical Center.[[234]](#cite_note-240)

#### Highways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Highways")]

Three of Wisconsin's [Interstate highways](/wiki/Interstate_highway "Interstate highway") intersect in Milwaukee. [Interstate 94](/wiki/Interstate_94_in_Wisconsin "Interstate 94 in Wisconsin") (I-94) comes north from Chicago to enter Milwaukee and continues west to [Madison](/wiki/Madison,_Wisconsin "Madison, Wisconsin"). The stretch of I-94 from Seven Mile Road to the [Marquette Interchange](/wiki/Marquette_Interchange "Marquette Interchange") in Downtown Milwaukee is known as the North-South Freeway. I-94 from downtown Milwaukee west to Wisconsin 16 is known as the East-West Freeway.

[I-43](/wiki/I-43 "I-43") enters Milwaukee from [Beloit](/wiki/Beloit,_Wisconsin "Beloit, Wisconsin") in the southwest and continues north along Lake Michigan to [Green Bay](/wiki/Green_Bay,_Wisconsin "Green Bay, Wisconsin") via [Sheboygan](/wiki/Sheboygan,_Wisconsin "Sheboygan, Wisconsin") and [Manitowoc](/wiki/Manitowoc,_Wisconsin "Manitowoc, Wisconsin"). I-43 southwest of I-41/I-894/US 41/US 45 [Hale Interchange](/wiki/Hale_Interchange "Hale Interchange") is known as the Rock Freeway. I-43 is cosigned with I-894 East and I-41/US 41 South to I-94 is known as the Airport Freeway. At I-94, I-43 follows I-94 to the Marquette Interchange. I-43 continues north known as the North-South Freeway to Wisconsin Highway 57 near Port Washington.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Milwaukee_May_2024_2_%28Hoan_Bridge%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_May_2024_2_%28Hoan_Bridge%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_May_2024_2_(Hoan_Bridge).jpg)

The [Hoan Bridge](/wiki/Hoan_Bridge "Hoan Bridge") carries [Interstate 794](/wiki/Interstate_794 "Interstate 794").

Approved in 2015, [Interstate 41](/wiki/Interstate_41 "Interstate 41") follows I-94 north from the state line before turning west at the [Mitchell Interchange](/wiki/Mitchell_Interchange "Mitchell Interchange") to the Hale Interchange and then north to Green Bay via [Fond du Lac](/wiki/Fond_du_Lac,_Wisconsin "Fond du Lac, Wisconsin"), [Oshkosh](/wiki/Oshkosh,_Wisconsin "Oshkosh, Wisconsin") and [Appleton](/wiki/Appleton,_Wisconsin "Appleton, Wisconsin"). I-41/US 41/US 45 from the Hale Interchange to Wisconsin Hwy 145 is known as the Zoo Freeway.

Milwaukee has two auxiliary Interstate Highways, [I-894](/wiki/I-894 "I-894") and [I-794](/wiki/I-794 "I-794"). I-894 bypasses downtown Milwaukee on the west and south sides of the city from the [Zoo Interchange](/wiki/Zoo_Interchange "Zoo Interchange") to the Mitchell Interchange. I-894 is part of the Zoo Freeway and the Airport Freeway. I-794 extends east from the Marquette Interchange to Lake Michigan before turning south over the [Hoan Bridge](/wiki/Hoan_Bridge "Hoan Bridge") toward Milwaukee Mitchell International Airport, turning into [Highway 794](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_794 "Wisconsin Highway 794") along the way. This is known as the Lake Freeway.

Milwaukee is also served by three [US Highways](/wiki/US_Highways "US Highways"). [U.S. Highway 18](/wiki/U.S._Route_18_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 18 in Wisconsin") (US 18) provides a link from downtown to points west heading to [Waukesha](/wiki/Waukesha,_Wisconsin "Waukesha, Wisconsin") along Wells Street, 17th/16th Streets, Highland Avenue, 35th Street, Wisconsin Avenue, and Blue Mound Road. [US 41](/wiki/U.S._Route_41_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 41 in Wisconsin") and [US 45](/wiki/U.S._Route_45_in_Wisconsin "U.S. Route 45 in Wisconsin") both provide north–south freeway transportation on the western side of the city. The freeway system in Milwaukee carries roughly 25% of all travel in Wisconsin.[[235]](#cite_note-241) Milwaukee County is also served by several [Wisconsin highways](/wiki/Wisconsin_highways "Wisconsin highways"), namely [24](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_24 "Wisconsin Highway 24"), [32](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_32 "Wisconsin Highway 32"), [36](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_36 "Wisconsin Highway 36"), [38](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_38 "Wisconsin Highway 38"), [57](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_57 "Wisconsin Highway 57"), [59](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_59 "Wisconsin Highway 59"), [100](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_100 "Wisconsin Highway 100"), [119](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_119 "Wisconsin Highway 119"), [145](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_145 "Wisconsin Highway 145"), [175](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_175 "Wisconsin Highway 175"), [181](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_181 "Wisconsin Highway 181"), [190](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_190 "Wisconsin Highway 190"), [241](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_241 "Wisconsin Highway 241"), and [794](/wiki/Wisconsin_Highway_794 "Wisconsin Highway 794").

#### Bicycling

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Bicycling")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Oak_Leaf_Trail_Milwaukee_River_Line_October_2022_2.jpg/250px-Oak_Leaf_Trail_Milwaukee_River_Line_October_2022_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oak_Leaf_Trail_Milwaukee_River_Line_October_2022_2.jpg)

The [Oak Leaf Trail](/wiki/Oak_Leaf_Trail "Oak Leaf Trail"), pictured on the [East Side](/wiki/East_Side,_Milwaukee "East Side, Milwaukee"), connects parks in the Milwaukee County Park System.

As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit), Milwaukee has 195 miles (314 km) of on-street bicycle facilities, including various kinds of bicycle lanes, bicycle boulevards, and trails. In the following year, the city set a goal of increasing their [protected bicycle lanes](/wiki/Protected_bicycle_lane "Protected bicycle lane") from 2.6 miles (4.2 km) to 50 miles (80 km) by 2026.[[236]](#cite_note-242)

In 2006, Milwaukee obtained bronze-level status from the League of American Bicyclists,[[237]](#cite_note-243) a rarity for a city its size,[[238]](#cite_note-244) then silver-level status in 2019.[[239]](#cite_note-245)

The Bicycle Federation of Wisconsin[[240]](#cite_note-246) holds an annual Bike to Work Week. The event, held in May each year, has frequently featured a commuter race between a car, a bus, and a bike; and also a morning ride into work with the mayor.

In 2008, the city identified over 250 miles (400 km) of streets on which bike lanes will fit. It created a plan labeling 145 miles (233 km) of those as high priority for receiving bike lanes.[[241]](#cite_note-247) As part of the city's Bicycle and Pedestrian Task Force's mission to "make Milwaukee more bicycle and pedestrian friendly", as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit), over 700 bike racks have been installed throughout the city.[[242]](#cite_note-248) Since October 2018, when it enacted a [complete streets](/wiki/Complete_streets "Complete streets") policy, the city continuously considers the addition of bicycle facilities to roadways as part of new road projects.[[243]](#cite_note-249)

In 2009, the [Milwaukee County Transit System](/wiki/Milwaukee_County_Transit_System "Milwaukee County Transit System") began installing bicycle racks to the front of county buses.[[244]](#cite_note-250) This "[green](/wiki/Environmental_movement "Environmental movement")" effort was part of a settlement of an [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") lawsuit filed by the state against the county in 2006.[[245]](#cite_note-251) The lawsuit cites the release of asbestos into the environment when the [Courthouse Annex](/wiki/Courthouse_Annex "Courthouse Annex") was demolished.[[246]](#cite_note-252)

In August 2014, Milwaukee debuted a [bicycle sharing system](/wiki/Bicycle_sharing_system "Bicycle sharing system") called [Bublr Bikes](/wiki/Bublr_Bikes "Bublr Bikes"), which is a partnership between the City of Milwaukee and a local non-profit, Midwest Bike Share (dba Bublr Bikes).[[247]](#cite_note-253)[[248]](#cite_note-254) As of August 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit), the system operates over 100 stations in the city and neighboring West Allis and Wauwatosa.[[249]](#cite_note-255)

#### Water

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Water")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_23_%28McKinley_Marina%29.jpg/250px-Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_23_%28McKinley_Marina%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Milwaukee_Boat_Line_tour_July_2022_23_(McKinley_Marina).jpg)

[McKinley Marina](/wiki/McKinley_Marina "McKinley Marina")

Milwaukee's main port, the [Port of Milwaukee](/wiki/Port_of_Milwaukee "Port of Milwaukee"), handled 2.3 million metric tons of cargo through its municipal port in 2022.[[250]](#cite_note-256) Commodities handled include salt, steel, limestone, general cargoes, over-dimensional cargoes, grain, fertilizers, biodiesel, and ethanol.[[251]](#cite_note-257) The port is a key ferry and cruise ship destination on Lake Michigan. It is home to the [Lake Express](/wiki/Lake_Express "Lake Express") ferry, which offers service from Milwaukee to [Muskegon, Michigan](/wiki/Muskegon,_Michigan "Muskegon, Michigan").[[168]](#cite_note-:0-174) The [United States Coast Guard Sector Lake Michigan](/wiki/United_States_Coast_Guard_Sector#List_of_Coast_Guard_Sectors "United States Coast Guard Sector") is based at the port.[[168]](#cite_note-:0-174) [McKinley Marina](/wiki/McKinley_Marina "McKinley Marina") is the city's only public marina.[[252]](#cite_note-258)

### City development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: City development")]

On February 10, 2015, a streetcar connecting the Milwaukee Intermodal Station with the city's [Lower East Side](/wiki/The_East_Side_(Milwaukee) "The East Side (Milwaukee)") was approved by the Common Council, bringing decades of sometimes acrimonious debate to a pause. On a 9–6 vote, the council approved a measure that established the project's $124 million capital budget, its estimated $3.2 million operating and maintenance budget and its 2.5-mile (4.0 km) route, which includes a lakefront spur connecting the line to the proposed $122 million, 44-story Couture. Construction on the Milwaukee Streetcar began March 2017, with initial operation by mid-2018.[[230]](#cite_note-fox6now-236)[[253]](#cite_note-259) This project was later named to The Hop, and became a free transit system.[[254]](#cite_note-JS-2017oct-260)[[255]](#cite_note-WITI-2017Oct-261) The Lakefront service was expected to start operation by 2019.[[230]](#cite_note-fox6now-236)

In 2024, *[Architectural Digest](/wiki/Architectural_Digest "Architectural Digest")* rated Milwaukee's skyline as the 15th most beautiful skyline in the world.[[256]](#cite_note-262) Part of that skyline includes the [Northwestern Mutual Tower and Commons](/wiki/Northwestern_Mutual_Tower_and_Commons "Northwestern Mutual Tower and Commons") stands 550 feet (170 m) tall and has 32 stories, making it the second tallest building in Milwaukee.[[257]](#cite_note-263)[[258]](#cite_note-264)

[Fiserv Forum](/wiki/Fiserv_Forum "Fiserv Forum"), a new multipurpose arena at 1111 Vel R. Phillips Avenue, has been built to accommodate the [Milwaukee Bucks](/wiki/Milwaukee_Bucks "Milwaukee Bucks") and [Marquette Golden Eagles](/wiki/Marquette_Golden_Eagles_men%27s_basketball "Marquette Golden Eagles men's basketball"), as well as college and professional ice hockey games. Construction on the $524 million project began in November 2015 and opened to the public on August 26, 2018.[[259]](#cite_note-265) The arena is intended to be the focal point of a "live block" zone that includes public space surrounded by both commercial and residential developments. The arena has a transparent facade and a curved roof and side that is meant to evoke the water forms of nearby Lake Michigan and the [Milwaukee River](/wiki/Milwaukee_River "Milwaukee River").[[260]](#cite_note-266)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

See also: [List of people from Milwaukee](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Milwaukee "List of people from Milwaukee")

* The American [sitcom](/wiki/Sitcom "Sitcom") *[Happy Days](/wiki/Happy_Days "Happy Days")* was set in Milwaukee and ran for 11 seasons from 1974 to 1984, becoming one of the most successful sitcoms in American television history. It presented an idealized vision of life in the 1950s and early-1960s [Midwestern United States](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States").
* The American sitcom, *[Laverne & Shirley](/wiki/Laverne_%26_Shirley "Laverne & Shirley")*, a spin-off of *Happy Days*, which played for eight seasons on ABC from January 27, 1976, to May 10, 1983, followed the lives of Laverne DeFazio and Shirley Feeney, two friends and roommates who work as bottle-cappers in the fictitious Shotz Brewery in late 1950s Milwaukee.
* The 2004 sports comedy film, *[Mr. 3000](/wiki/Mr._3000 "Mr. 3000"),* takes place in Milwaukee and features actor [Bernie Mac](/wiki/Bernie_Mac "Bernie Mac") as a member of the [Milwaukee Brewers](/wiki/Milwaukee_Brewers "Milwaukee Brewers").[[261]](#cite_note-267)
* In the 1992 movie, [*Wayne's World*](/wiki/Wayne%27s_World_(film) "Wayne's World (film)"), the two main characters, Wayne and Garth, meet rock star [Alice Cooper](/wiki/Alice_Cooper "Alice Cooper") after a show in Milwaukee. Cooper engages in a discussion with them and his band about Milwaukee and where the city's name comes from.[[262]](#cite_note-268)[[263]](#cite_note-269)
* The 2011 comedy film [*Bridesmaids*](/wiki/Bridesmaids_(2011_film) "Bridesmaids (2011 film)") starring [Kristen Wiig](/wiki/Kristen_Wiig "Kristen Wiig"), [Maya Rudolph](/wiki/Maya_Rudolph "Maya Rudolph") and [Rebel Wilson](/wiki/Rebel_Wilson "Rebel Wilson") had multiple scenes set in Milwaukee, though it was filmed in California.[[264]](#cite_note-270)
* The headquarters of film and video production company [Red Letter Media](/wiki/Red_Letter_Media "Red Letter Media") are in Milwaukee. The company often discuss its pride in the city.[[265]](#cite_note-271)
* The 2016 documentary film *[MILWAUKEE 53206](/wiki/Milwaukee_53206 "Milwaukee 53206")*, directed by Keith McQuirter, examines the impact of mass incarceration by following the lives of families in Milwaukee's 53206 ZIP code, which at the time of production had one of the highest incarceration rates for African-American men in the United States.[[266]](#cite_note-272)[[267]](#cite_note-273) The film received national attention for its portrayal of the toll that incarceration takes on communities and had its national broadcast premiere on [WORLD Channel](/wiki/World_Channel "World Channel")'s *[America ReFramed](/wiki/America_ReFramed "America ReFramed")* series.[[268]](#cite_note-274)[[269]](#cite_note-275)
* The 2025 novel *[Shadow Ticket](/wiki/Shadow_Ticket "Shadow Ticket")* by [Thomas Pynchon](/wiki/Thomas_Pynchon "Thomas Pynchon") is set in Milwaukee, following private investigator Hicks McTaggart, whose firm assigns him to find an heiress of a large [Wisconsin cheese](/wiki/Wisconsin_cheese "Wisconsin cheese") fortune.[[270]](#cite_note-276)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Milwaukee&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Notes")]

Both areas were annexed to Milwaukee for industrial reasons; the Waukesha County portion contains a [Cargill](/wiki/Cargill "Cargill") plant for Ambrosia Chocolate (known as "the Ambrosia triangle"), while the Washington County portion contains a [Waste Management](/wiki/Waste_Management_(corporation) "Waste Management (corporation)") facility.[[75]](#cite_note-foran-counties-76)
